articles.title: Predictors of Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Recurrence After Release Surgery: A Narrative Review of Clinical and Surgical Strategies to Reduce Risk
articles.authors: Moein Mirzai; Mohammad Hossein Nabian; Seyyed Ali Alavi Khoshhall
articles.abstract:
Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) represents the most frequently encountered peripheral nerve entrapment in clinical practice, and carpal tunnel release (CTR) remains the primary intervention for symptomatic relief. Although most patients experience substantial improvement, a subset continue to experience persistent or recurrent symptoms, highlighting the importance of understanding the factors associated with treatment failure.
This review synthesizes current evidence on predictors of symptom recurrence following CTR. Differences among surgical techniques, including open CTR (OCTR), endoscopic CTR (ECTR), and mini-incision CTR (MCTR), are examined in relation to incomplete decompression and postsurgical scar formation. Patient-related factors such as age, sex, smoking status, and hand dominance are discussed alongside common comorbidities, including diabetes mellitus (DM), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and hypertension (HTN). Emerging evidence regarding genetic susceptibility and molecular mechanisms involved in fibrosis and tissue remodeling is also explored. Additionally, the potential role of diagnostic modalities such as ultrasound (US), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and electromyography (EMG) in identifying patients at increased risk of recurrence is evaluated.
Overall, the findings emphasize the importance of individualized patient assessment and tailored surgical decision-making. Integrating multiple prognostic factors may support optimized surgical strategies and improve long-term outcomes following CTR. Additionally, the review provided a graphical abstract to illustrate the main prognostic factors involved in CTS recurrence

articles.title: Label-Framing and Organizational Control: How Memes and Narratives Shape Workplace Communication
articles.authors: Koichi Hiraoka
articles.abstract:
This study examines how everyday language functions not merely as a medium of 
communication, but as a structural mechanism of organizational control that shapes 
cognition, emotion, behavior, recognition, and cultural reproduction within workplaces. 
Previous studies on labeling theory, framing theory, speech act theory, meme theory, 
narrative theory, and organizational silence have largely developed as separate research 
traditions. However, limited attention has been paid to how these processes interact to 
constrain speakability and behavioral possibility in organizational environments. 
To address this gap, this paper introduces the concept of Label-Framing, defined as a 
mutually reinforcing process in which social labels and interpretive frames recursively 
stabilize meanings attached to persons, roles, and behaviors. Labels categorize 
individuals, while frames assign normative meanings to those categories. Through 
repeated organizational communication, Label-Framing shapes expectations regarding 
who may speak, how one should behave, which emotions are legitimate, and what 
actions become cognitively available or unavailable. 
The study argues that organizational control often operates not through explicit coercion, 
but through the internalization of repeated linguistic classifications. As Label-Framing 
intensifies, externally imposed labels may become integrated into self-description itself, 
transforming organizational discourse into a mechanism of self-regulation and adaptive 
silence. 
Drawing on anonymized comparative observations of contrasting communication 
environments within the same organization, the paper shows that high Label-Framing 
environments tend to produce self-censorship, silence, emotional homogenization, 
recognition dependency, and reduced cognitive flexibility. In contrast, low 
Label-Framing environments are more likely to sustain dialogue, humor, contextual 
interpretation, relational repair, and psychological safety. The analysis suggests that 
organizational culture is reproduced not only through formal systems or institutional 
rules, but also through repeated labels, frames, emotional expressions, and narratives. 
The theoretical contribution of this study is to reconceptualize organizational control as 
communicative stabilization rather than merely formal authority or institutional 
discipline. By integrating labeling, framing, speech acts, emotional memes, narrative 
reproduction, and organizational silence into a single communicative process, this paper 
offers a framework for understanding how language structures organizational reality. It 
also suggests that organizational reform requires not only institutional redesign, but 
critical reflection on which linguistic patterns, emotional expressions, and interpretive 
frameworks are repeatedly legitimized in everyday workplace communication.

articles.title: Detecting Divisive Language: A Concept-Grounded, LLM-Guided Pipeline for Polarizing Social Media Sphere
articles.authors: Yuting He; Tianhao Li; Jiebiao Wang; Yongjun Zhang
articles.abstract:
Political polarization poses a growing global challenge, yet existing NLP approaches typically rely on indirect proxies such as toxicity or negative sentiment, which fail to capture identity-based antagonism that is central to polarizing discourse. We address this gap by conceptualizing identity-related polarization discourse as divisive language: language that explains political or social disagreement by attributing it to group-based identities. Building on this definition, we propose a staged training pipeline that uses large language
models (LLMs) to generate definition-grounded supervision and progressively distills it into lightweight classifiers suitable for large-scale analysis. Experiments on social media data show that the resulting models substantially outperform zero-shot prompting and small-scale supervised baselines, while detecting forms of polarization that are not captured by toxicity- or sentiment-based methods. Our findings demonstrate that divisive language can be treated as a distinct, computable linguistic construct, enabling scalable and theoretically grounded analysis of political polarization.

articles.title: Italian Brainrot as a GenAI Meme: The Evolution of Slop and Brainrot Aesthetics in the Digital Cultural Economy.
articles.authors: Lucie Chateau
articles.abstract: Brainrot is a meme genre that collects the detritus of the internet, both aesthetically and intellectually speaking. It is a catch-all term for low-quality content that is produced online in an attempt to claim a stake in the attention economy. Slop is a term that has gained traction in the last year as a way to refer to the constant and overwhelming output of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI). Slop is engaged with lazily, sloppily and spontaneously, not created with the aim of embodying a particular aesthetic mode, but rather mimicking others. Recently, both terms have collided during the viral internet meme genre of “Italian Brainrot”. However, brainrot and slop stand in completely different aesthetic lineages and thus represent completely different modes of production and distribution. This article provides an analysis of the evolution of brainrot and slop as aesthetic phenomena by placing them in the context of meme culture and expanding on their role in the digital economy. It argues that the difference between brainrot and slop is crucial to make going forward, as we see more and more slop content being circulated by both platforms and users. Spam has become the key logic of production in this GenAI-fuelled slop economy, which threatens to flatten and homogenise cultural expression. Brainrot content in meme culture, however, is presented here as the antithesis of the slop economy due to its aesthetic creativity and ongoing acts of cultural remediation.

articles.title: Forcing generalization: technical art as (synthetic) data work
articles.authors: James Steinhoff
articles.abstract: Synthetic data has recently been proposed as an alternate means of procuring data for training AI which dispenses with data work. However the labour required to produce it has not been studied. This paper does so by looking at the technical artist: a hybrid programmer and 3D artist recently brought into the AI industry from the games and film industry. I argue that technical art, in the synthetic data context, is data work but of an unfamiliar kind. I demonstrate this through a labour process analysis of procedural asset creation. I show that in the synthetic data context, technical art is governed by the goal of forcing generalization. I suggest that the concept of data work should not ossify to capture only its present state of collection and cleaning, but that a more mutable concept is necessary to track changes in the AI industry. While claims of data work’s coming disappearance are implausible, it seems unwise to overstate its permanency in its present state.

articles.title: Firework Dominance Illusion:  How Hierarchical Communication Distorts the Perceived Severity of Workplace Harassment
articles.authors: Koichi Hiraoka
articles.abstract:
This paper theorizes Firework Dominance Illusion (FDI), a communication-based 
phenomenon in which the same harassment behavior is perceived as severe by affected 
parties on site but minimized by upper management because of hierarchical distance. 
Using the metaphor of aerial fireworks, the paper argues that harassment, like fireworks, 
is experienced differently depending on the observer’s position: from the ground, it is 
accompanied by sound, vibration, fear, and bodily impact, whereas from above it 
appears only as a small point of light. In workplace harassment, this difference produces 
a communicative distortion between those who experience intimidation, silence, 
exclusion, and information blocking, and those who interpret the same events as strict 
guidance, localized friction, or ordinary workplace management. 
The paper connects this model with research on organizational communication, 
employee voice and silence, psychological safety, abusive supervision, toxic leadership, 
sensemaking, and institutional responses to workplace conflict. It argues that recurrence 
prevention often fails because upper management receives only filtered or diminished 
signals from the ground-level workplace. Victim reports are therefore treated as 
subjective or emotional complaints, while the perpetrator’s competence, politeness 
toward superiors, and symbolic organizational value may be overestimated. 
By reframing workplace harassment as a problem of hierarchical communication and 
perceptual distortion, the paper explains why harm that is obvious at the ground level 
may remain underestimated at higher levels of the organization. The Firework 
Dominance Illusion model suggests that effective prevention requires not only 
fact-finding and individual correction, but also mechanisms for translating ground-level 
communicative experience into managerial understanding before silence, exclusion, and 
turnover reproduce the same harm.

articles.title: A Structural-Semiotic Analysis of the Comb Sign M176 in the Indus Script as a Referent Category Classifier - A Hypothesis
articles.authors: Inderjeet Singh
articles.abstract:
This article gives a structural-semiotic analysis of the comb sign (Sign 176 in Mahadevan's 1977 concordance) in the Indus script, and advances the methodological argument that semiotics constitutes a necessary complement to epigraphy in the study of undeciphered scripts. Epigraphy asks what a sign says in the language it encodes, on the other hand Semiotics asks what a sign does in the sign system, a question that can be answered without phonological decipherment. Drawing on Sampson (1985), Harris (1986, 2000), and Coulmas (1989), the article grounds this distinction theoretically and demonstrates it through three semiotic frameworks applied to the comb sign: Peirce’s triadic model of icon, index, and symbol; Saussure’s syntagmatic and paradigmatic analysis; and Barthes’ denotation, connotation, and myth. These frameworks support the hypothesis, first advanced by Mahadevan (1982, 2006, 2012), that Sign 176 probably functions as a referent category classifier, though the category it encodes could not be determined without decipherment.
The contributions of this article are of theoretical and methodological nature. It is perhaps the first simultaneous application of all three classical semiotic frameworks to a single Indus sign. Secondly, only a few previous studies appear to have formulated explicitly the methodological complementarity of semiotics and epigraphy in Indus script research, this article advances that thesis in grounded form. It also tries to offer what appears to be probably the first structured cross-cultural comparative analysis of classifier sign function across the Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Indus traditions on the basis of existing literature. Fourthly, it advances a new cross-linguistic cognitive motivation argument for the referent category classifier hypothesis. The article additionally suggests that the Indus sign system may have functioned as a professionally restricted administrative notation. No decipherment claims are advanced.

articles.title: Large Language Models for Statistical Analysis: Can they Replace Domain-Specific Software Packages?
articles.authors: David Ajayi
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) represent one of the most significant advances in artificial intelligence in recent decades.  Although LLMs are widely used to generate statistical code in domain-specific languages such as R, Python, and SAS, they are increasingly employed to perform data analysis directly. Prior studies have examined the use of LLMs in statistical analysis, but important gaps remain, including limited evidence on their proficiency in data manipulation and Bayesian statistical modeling. The present study was designed to evaluate the performance of common LLMs across a wide range of data analysis tasks, including data reading, data manipulation, descriptive statistics, contingency table analysis, mean comparison tests, correlation analysis, regression modeling, and Bayesian inference. Six large language models were assessed: ChatGPT 5.3, Gemini 3.1, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Microsoft Copilot GPT 5.1, Grok 4.2, and DeepSeek 3.2. All models were tested using their free-tier access, except for ChatGPT, which was evaluated through a paid subscription. Fully or partially simulated datasets were used in this study, and strict scoring criteria were implemented, in that the outputs from the LLMs must be consistent with those from R, and they must be reproducible upon re-run. Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude achieved 100% accuracy in data reading and descriptive statistics. Gemini and Claude generated correct results for mean comparison tests. ChatGPT and Claude produced accurate outputs in correlation and regression analyses. None of the LLMs achieved 100% accuracy in data manipulation, contingency table analyses, and Bayesian modeling. On average, no LLM achieved perfect accuracy. The overall performance of Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude was comparable, whereas Grok, Copilot, and DeepSeek performed poorly. Limitations in data manipulation and some inferential statistical methods suggest that LLMs cannot yet replace domain-specific software packages. Therefore, LLMs are better suited as complementary tools rather than standalone applications for rigorous statistical analysis.

articles.title: Women Overinvest for the Public Good, But Only When They Must Act Alone
articles.authors: Lexin Liang; Paul Glimcher
articles.abstract:
It has been robustly demonstrated that when conflicts arise between self-interest and collective
interest, individual women disproportionately sacrifice to benefit the collective. This tendency
is widely observed in workplace settings, where women take on “non-promotable” tasks that
benefit the organization more than the women who perform them. However, it remains unclear
exactly when and why women overinvest, and how best to mitigate the resulting inequity this
produces. To answer these questions, we developed an economic game in which a single
individual’s investment improves group outcomes at a cost to the ‘investor’
. We replicated the
canonical observation that women overinvest individually, but contrary to all standard
predictions we show that women invested more and earned less only when they were the sole
woman in a group. Our game-theoretic analysis supports the novel conclusion that women
adopt a distinct decision-making strategy when interacting with men. While this unique female
strategy we is efficient in groups with multiple women, both the data and model show that it
becomes uniquely costly when a woman acts alone in an otherwise male group. Women’s
tendency to overinvest does not reflect gender-specific preferences as generally assumed, but
rather arises as a strategic attempt to maximise their earnings while interacting as a group with
men. Our findings highlight the costs to women when institutions create groups that contain
only one woman – situations that systematically disadvantage solo women employing this
gender-aware strategy.

articles.title: One Lone Item: Assessing Loneliness with a Single-Item Direct Measure
articles.authors: Paddy Maher; Yavor Dragostinov; Ross David Stewart; René Mõttus; Andrew John Cooper; Elliot M. Tucker-Drob
articles.abstract: Across the most-used loneliness scales, direct wordings are avoided due to concerns they induce biased responses. These efforts have undermined scales’ abilities to accurately assess loneliness. We test and validate the One-item Loneliness scale (1iL; "I often feel lonely"), showing using multi-rater data (Ntotal = 2,638 people) that item directness does not induce unique biases. The 1iL had retest reliability superior to some short multi-item scales (rtt = .74) and comparable cross-rater agreement (rSOA = .52). We find near-perfect convergence with the Three-Item Loneliness Scale (rtrue = .95), the most popular short measure of loneliness, and the Emotional subscale of the De Jong Gierveld (DJG; rtrue = .97). Crucially, the 1iL demonstrated clear discriminant validity from the DJG's Social subscale, suggesting that ‘social loneliness’ is in fact distinct from loneliness. Contrary to popular belief, loneliness can be assessed directly, and is psychologically distinct from judgements about one’s social circle.

articles.title: An introduction to rational constructivism in cognitive development
articles.authors: Rebekah A. Gelpí; Pablo Leon-Villagra; Daphna Buchsbaum; Stephanie Denison
articles.abstract: Rational constructivism is a contemporary theory of cognitive development. It proposes that children generate, tweak and radically revise theories, beliefs, and representations by integrating evidence with existing knowledge via probabilistic inferential learning mechanisms. We begin with a general overview of the theory of rational constructivism, covering its theoretical commitments and predictions. We then describe how it accounts for the empirically observed patterns of both incremental and radical developmental change and discuss the cognitive mechanisms underlying those conceptual changes. Finally, we sketch out a general computational framework for it and address open questions and directions for future research.

articles.title: Mapping Well-Being Science: A Bibliometric Review of Its Evolution, Structure, and Future Directions
articles.authors: Jaymes Paolo Rombaoa; Saida Heshmati
articles.abstract: We used bibliometric methods to comprehensively map the intellectual structure of well-being research in the psychological sciences. We used a database of over 30,000 primary documents downloaded from Web of Science and leveraged three bibliometric methods: historiography, document co-citation analysis, and bibliographic coupling. The findings shed light on the (1) evolution of well-being science over time, (2) the underlying structure of the intellectual field and its current state, and (3) the future trajectory of the field and emerging topics. Based on our findings, we provide three future directions for well-being science: (i) embracing diversity and broadening the scope of well-being scholarship, (ii) transcending beyond dichotomous perspectives of well-being, and (iii) harnessing advanced methods and measures for a stronger scientific foundation. This paper serves as a valuable resource for researchers, policymakers, and practitioners and provides guidance and direction in addressing the challenges related to well-being scholarship.

articles.title: Epigenetic signatures of different types of childhood maltreatment in a high-risk sample: a preregistered replication study
articles.authors: Maria Meier; Laura Gurri; Katharina Beck; Delfine d'Huart; Philippe Jawinski; Cyril Boonmann; Christina Stadler; Marc Schmid; David Bürgin; Eva Unternaehrer
articles.abstract:
Childhood maltreatment (CM) has long-lasting effects on physical and mental health with CM type specific effects being reported. Epigenetic processes, like deoxyribonucleic acid methylation (DNAm), might mediate this link, and CM types might be linked to differential alterations in DNAm. While a study by Cecil et al. (2016) suggests that different forms of CM share common but also exhibit distinct epigenetic signatures, it is unclear whether these results are generalizable to other high-risk samples. 
The aim of this preregistered study was to replicate the study of Cecil and colleagues (2016) in a sample of N = 116 previously out-of-home placed young adults (32% women, age mean = 26.38, SD = 3.49). Participants completed the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire - Short Form (CTQ-SF), DNA was extracted from whole blood and DNAm was assayed using the Illumina Infinium MethylationEPIC BeadChip Microarray. 
Using state-of the-art processing pipelines, we failed to replicate the original finding of common and shared epigenetic signatures by CM type. When using a pipeline that was closely matched to the original study, we could replicate that DNAm of cg07032930 was linked to emotional neglect, but in the reverse direction as in the original report. Overall, replication rates per CM subtype were only modest. 
These findings highlight challenges in independently replicating reported epigenetic associations in high-risk samples and underscore the importance of replication attempts in the field of behavioral epigenetics. Further research is needed to clarify the robustness and specificity of DNAm signatures linked to distinct CM types.

articles.title: Toward a Multi-Layered Pattern Interpretation Framework  for Digital ADHD Screening: Integrating Self-Report,  Symptom Structuring, Response Inhibition, and Sustained  Attention Tasks
articles.authors: Kyungmin OH; Min Seo Kim; Yerim Choi; Soo Hyeon Kim; Hyo Joo Shin; Suyeon Lim; Yumin Jeon
articles.abstract:
Digital ADHD screening frameworks may help organize self-report and task-based information relevant to ADHD-related difficulties, but such outputs may be overinterpreted when reduced to a single questionnaire score, behavioral index, or automated risk judgment. This conceptual paper proposes a multi-layered pattern interpretation framework for digital ADHD screening that integrates self-report questionnaire responses, DSM-5-informed symptom structuring, a Go/No-Go response inhibition task, and a CPT-type sustained attention task. The main contribution of the framework is to treat agreement and disagreement between subjective reports and behavioral task indicators as the primary object of interpretation. In particular, disagreement is not regarded as measurement failure, but as potentially meaningful information about the difference between everyday difficulties and performance in structured task contexts.
The framework describes four representative patterns: convergent difficulty, subjective difficulty-dominant, performance instability-dominant, and low-signal patterns. These patterns are intended to organize self-understanding-related information and indicate areas that may require contextual examination, not to classify individuals or diagnose ADHD. The manuscript also defines ethical boundaries, including the avoidance of diagnostic claims, transparency about the lack of clinical validation, responsible handling of mental health-related data, and careful interpretive wording. No human participant data were collected, analyzed, or reported, and the framework does not provide evidence of reliability, validity, diagnostic accuracy, or clinical applicability. Future empirical studies with appropriate ethical review are required to examine interpretability, ethical safety, measurement reliability, construct validity, and relationships with clinical assessment. The proposed framework reframes digital ADHD screening as cautious, contextual, and ethically bounded pattern interpretation rather than automated classification. This framework is proposed as a conceptual interpretive model, not as a user-facing product, intervention, referral system, or clinical decision tool.

articles.title: Autonomic Nervous System Dysregulation in Psychopathology: Hyperadrenergic Dysautonomia
articles.authors: Farhad Montazeri
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This study explores hemodynamic measures for patterns of autonomic system dysfunction in correlation with psychopathology, in a large diverse sample of Adolescents. 

Methods: 10,191 subjects from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, who were simultaneously measured for psychopathology, with Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), and hemodynamic status (resting vital signs) were selected. Resting heart rate positively correlated across CBCL scales, with a diurnal pattern.  The arousal windows were identified by diurnal sensitivity analysis. Hierarchical Clustering Analysis of subjects measured within these windows identified other hemodynamic markers that clustered with the heart rate, following its correlational pattern with CBCL scales. Next, we calculated the Fractional Hyperadrenergic Index (FHI), a composite measure of these markers, weighted for compensatory tachycardia and standardized for sex and explored its association with "Total Problems" scale of CBCL (a measure of the total load of psychopathology based on the p-factor model), while controlling for demographic covariates, through bootstrapped Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression analysis 

Results: A distinct bimodal diurnal rhythm was found in the correlation between heart rate and psychopathology, peaking in early morning and late afternoon. We found a psychopathology-correlated pattern of "hyperadrenergic" autonomic dysfunction, with narrowed pulse pressure, and compensatory tachycardia, indicative of preload reduction, and mirroring hyperadrenergic Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS), more prominently in males. Bootstrapped OLS regression found FHI a highly significant, independent predictor of the psychopathology load (β = 0.79, p < .001). 

Conclusion: Synaptic level Norepinephrine (NE) function appears to be the plausible (and inevitable) link between autonomic and psychological dysregulation. Exploration of blood and urinary levels of NE and its metabolites, especially during the arousal windows, could yield correlates of the load of psychopathology. Importantly since NE is also involved in derangements of other systems, commonly co-occurring with psychopathology—i.e. (auto) inflammation—we propose a paradigm shift towards a “∑-factor model”, a broader perspective, that integrate p-factor psychopathology with other factorial dimensions, representing other organ-system involvement, in a model including multiple intertwined organ-system derangements, resulting from the complex interplay of etiologies that cross the boundaries of psychology, autonomic nervous system (hyper-adrenergic state), immune system(T-cell dysfunction), etc., and affect them all, for example via neuro-immune axis dysregulation.

articles.title: Immune system dysregulation in Psychopathology: Insights from the Complete Blood Count (CBC)
articles.authors: Farhad Montazeri
articles.abstract:
We explored biological correlates of psychopathology in 1,229 subjects of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, who completed both Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL 6-18) and a set of laboratory tests (Complete Blood Count, High-Density Lipoprotein, total cholesterol, and ferritin). An Ordinary Least Squares model identified independent associations between the CBCL 'Total Problems' scale, as a measure of psychopathology load, and the first factor of the clusters formed by the laboratory tests: 'inflammation' and 'anemia'. 

In the 'inflammation' cluster, platelet count, white blood cells, monocyte and neutrophil counts, lymphocyte percentage, and HDL correlated with CBCL scales. Markers of neutrophilic inflammation correlated stronger with the 'internalizing' dimension, while male sex correlated with the 'externalizing' dimension. Erythropoiesis negatively correlated with several scales across both. Eosinophils correlated with ADHD. The 'Total Problems' scale was independently associated with the 'Inflammation factor' (β = 0.081, CI [0.023, 0.139]), the 'Anemia factor' (β = -0.070, CI [-0.129, -0.014]), and male sex (β= 0.199, CI [0.085, 0.310]).   

Background (auto) inflammation, anemia (likely secondary to the former), and male sex are robust correlates of the psychopathology load. This study sheds light at the biological processes involved in dimensional psychopathology and can improve detection and inform future interventions.

articles.title: SCOPE: The Generative Architecture of Cultural Learning
articles.authors: Francys Subiaul
articles.abstract: Humans transform observations into increasingly complex tools, practices, and ideas. Social learning (SL) research has explained this capacity using familiar constructs such as imitation, emulation, overimitation, teaching, and innovation. This focus has been productive, but it leaves a central puzzle unresolved: populations that share many learning capacities nevertheless differ sharply in cultural accumulation and innovation. These differences suggest that cumulative culture depends not only on individual mechanisms, but how they are coordinated and deployed across individuals, populations, and generations. This article proposes SCOPE (Segment → Choose → Operate → Pool → Expand), an architecture specifying how socially available information becomes cumulative cultural knowledge. Segment parses demonstrations and testimony into structured representations of who did what, where, when, how, and why. Choose selects among competing interpretations, responses, or learning strategies. Operate retrieves and deploys selected representations in action, explanation, or inference. Pool integrates information across episodes, models, and contexts. Expand generalizes, and recombines representations into new forms. SCOPE predicts broad continuity in early representational and control operations, but sharper human–ape discontinuities in Pool and Expand. The framework offers a testable account of developmental, cultural, and species-level variation in cultural learning.

articles.title: Replicating Partisan Differences in Partisan Media Data
articles.authors: Kayla Nicole Jordan; William Edward Padfield; Jomana Abdelrahman; Erin Michelle Buchanan
articles.abstract: Partisan polarization in the United States extends beyond policy disagreements to the ways liberals and conservatives use language. Building on prior large-scale studies of political communication, we conducted a preregistered replication of hypothesized ideological differences across multiple linguistic domains, using news, congressional, and Twitter corpora. We applied multilevel modeling and bootstrapped effect size estimation to compare liberal and conservative sources across six domains: values, pronouns, linguistic style, socio-emotional expression, temporal focus, and motivational language. Results revealed robust and replicable partisan differences in values, pronoun use, and socio-emotional language, aligning with theories of motivated social cognition and prior findings that conservatives emphasize order, tradition, and threat while liberals emphasize openness, benevolence, and complexity. In contrast, hypothesized differences in temporal orientation and motivational framing proved less consistent, underscoring the role of situational context in shaping partisan communication. By integrating replication evidence across diverse data sources, this study clarifies which linguistic markers of ideology are stable psychological indicators and which are context-dependent. Our findings highlight the importance of replication for advancing cumulative science in political psychology and provide a more reliable foundation for future work at the intersection of language, ideology, and polarization.

articles.title: Processing complex spoken words with obscured morphological identity and boundary: An auditory masked priming experiment
articles.authors: Dave Kenneth Tayao Cayado; Samantha Wray; Adam J. Chong; Linnaea Stockall
articles.abstract: To comprehend speech, the auditory system segments a continuous input stream at various levels (e.g., sounds, words). Previous studies have shown that listeners also segment complex spoken words like treatment into their morphological units /ˈtriːt/+/mənt/, where there is a clear morphological boundary between /stem/+/affix/. Here, we report an auditory masked priming experiment in Tagalog investigating whether complex spoken words with obscured morphological identity and boundaries are segmented into morphemes. We compare the processing of prefixed words [1] without morphophonological alternations (NOCHANGE, e.g., /maŋ/+/híla/ becomes maŋhíla ‘to pull’), [2] with nasal ASSIMILATION obscuring prefix identity (e.g., /maŋ/+/búlag/ becomes mambúlag ‘to blind’), and [3] with nasal SUBSTITUTION obscuring both prefix and stem identities and their phonological boundary (e.g., /maŋ/+/túlak/ becomes manúlak ‘to push’). Crucially, these morphophonological alternations exhibit variability: nasal SUBSTITUTION is more likely for VOICELESS-initial stems than for VOICED-initial stems. We found significant priming effects of similar magnitude across NOCHANGE, ASSIMILATION, and SUBSTITUTION conditions. These effects are morphological in nature since no reliable priming was observed for monomorphemic word pairs with phonological overlap. Further, similar priming was found for both VOICELESS and VOICED SUBSTITUTION conditions. These findings unambiguously show that spoken complex words are decomposed into morphemes, thereby supporting auditory processing models and morphological theories which argue that morphologically complex words are stored and accessed as morphemes in the mental lexicon. Ultimately, we provide clear evidence that automatic decomposition in speech comprehension is robust in the face of variable morphophonological alternations that obscure prefix identity and morphological boundaries.

articles.title: Toward an ecology of emotion in everyday life
articles.authors: Ratna Kandala; Niels Vanhasbroeck; Bastiaan Tamm; Hugo Van hamme; Peter Kuppens; Batja Gomes Gomes de Mesquita; Katie Hoemann
articles.abstract: Human emotional life is rich and varied, yet psychological science often studies it in decontextualized ways, asking participants to rate feelings without capturing the situations that evoke them. Here, we map the ecology of everyday emotion by analyzing the link between affective experience and daily events. We collected thousands of real-time verbal descriptions of momentary affect from participants over 70 days. Using transformer-based language models, we identified granular topics corresponding to daily situations, from 'morning routines' to 'socializing with friends.' Our analysis revealed that the mapping between situations and emotions is not one-to-one, but many-to-many. For example, a single situation, such as 'reading books,' was associated with a wide spectrum of emotions, while a single emotion word, such as “angry,” appeared across a diverse range of situations. This data-driven approach moves beyond simple emotion labels to build a foundational ecology of human affective experience.

articles.title: For daily well-being, the context-specificity of emotion words matters as well as the variety
articles.authors: Ratna Kandala; Ali Faraji-Rad; Jeffrey M. Girard; Angelique Pershon; Vera Vine; Katie Hoemann
articles.abstract: Assigning labels to feelings facilitates access to scripts for what to expect, think, and do, with implications for well-being. Using a larger variety of emotion words suggests access to a wider range of scripts, whereas using more context-specific words suggests access to more precise scripts. It is unclear, however, whether these neighboring properties of emotion language—diversity and granularity—have unique relationships with well-being, and whether they reveal insights that cannot be learned from affective tone alone. We applied ecological frameworks and state-of-the-art large language models to natural language collected four times per day over 70 days from 102 Dutch-speakers in Belgium. Emotion word diversity and granularity were uniquely associated with momentary well-being beyond affective tone. Average granularity was also associated with trait well-being. Findings build on efforts to systematically investigate the role of emotion words in psychosocial functioning and advance the study of emotion language in daily life.

articles.title: Development of the Yoga Bali-A-TSA Program for Adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorder and Their Parents: Conceptual Framework.
articles.authors: Marie-Joelle Beaudoin; Gilles Dupuis; Nathalie Poirier
articles.abstract: The practice of yoga is gaining popularity as an intervention for children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Current studies on the feasibility and effectiveness of yoga programs support the benefits of this practice for this clinical population. However, the data remain preliminary and require further research. At present, limited information is provided about the theoretical constructs underpinning the studied yoga programs. This descriptive article aims to detail the conceptual framework that guided the development of the Yoga Bali ASD program for adolescents with autism spectrum disorder and their parents (YBP-A-ASD). Each component is described in relation to the theoretical constructs and scientific literature supporting its various elements. This article can serve as a guide for aligning the development of yoga programs for children and adolescents with ASD and their families with theoretical principles drawn from the scientific literature.

articles.title: Do I know what I want to say? Modeling meaning uncertainty in RSA
articles.authors: Anzi Wang; Carolyn Jane Anderson; Grusha Prasad
articles.abstract: Models using the Rational Speech Act (RSA) framework typically assume that speakers are certain about the meaning being communicated. In this work we note that there are contexts in which this assumption need not hold, and propose a method (um-RSA) to incorporate this meaning uncertainty within the RSA framework. As a case study, we explore two sources of meaning uncertainty: Counting-Uncertainty (from numerical cognition) and Discounting-Uncertainty (from behavioral economics). We generate predictions from these two hypotheses and test these predictions with two human experiments. The results show that um-RSA can account for differences in uncertainty expression usage that the standard RSA framework cannot account for, thus demonstrating the usefulness of modeling meaning uncertainty.

articles.title: Trauma Work as Emotional Labor: An Integrative Process Model
articles.authors: Andrea Fischbach
articles.abstract: Trauma work — repeated task-related confrontation with traumatic events or their aversive details as a core role requirement — is a recurring occupational hazard, yet work-specific mechanisms are under-specified in dominant clinical models of posttraumatic stress. This conceptual synthesis develops an integrative process model that differentiates extraordinary work-related trauma events (DSM-5 A1/A2) from ordinary job-specific exposure (DSM-5 A4). The model proposes three interdependent pathways through which exposure translates into trauma sequelae — PTSD, complex PTSD, burnout, and empathic distress: (1) autonomous stress responses, (2) controlled emotion regulation and emotional-labor processes, and (3) cumulative resource depletion through repeated demands and insufficient recovery. Task characteristics — duration, density, and intensity of exposure — define the trauma dose. Emotion regulation and self-regulation are the proximal moderators of these paths; they are themselves fueled by a controlled-process cascade running from job, social, and personal resources through role-breadth self-efficacy into the moderator paths. Depending on how organizations design and sustain these resources, the cascade either amplifies loss spirals or initiates gain spirals. Integrating an emotional labor climate perspective, the paper outlines workplace practices that reduce demands and strengthen resources at key points in the model. The paper identifies leverage points for organizational intervention and derives testable propositions for empirical work in trauma-exposed occupations.

articles.title: Region-Specific Brain Atrophy Across Alzheimer’s Disease Stages in the NACC Dataset
articles.authors: Noah St Clair; James Heeter; Luka Oushana
articles.abstract:
Alzheimer’s (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by widespread cognitive decline and structural brain atrophy. While hippocampal degeneration is a well-established hallmark of AD, increasing evidence suggests that patterns of atrophy vary substantially across brain regions and disease stages. Large-scale neuroimaging datasets provide an opportunity to characterize these anatomical changes at the population level and identify regions that may exhibit differential vulnerability during disease progression. 

In this study, we analyzed structural MRI-derived volumetric measurements from the National Alzheimer’s Coordinating Center (NACC) dataset to investigate region-specific atrophy across clinically defined stages of Alzheimer’s disease. Brain regions associated with memory, limbic processing, and cortical integration were normalized to estimated total intracranial volume (eTIV) to reduce variability related to head size. Comparisons were performed across diagnostic groups to evaluate the magnitude and progression of neuroanatomical degeneration. 

Our findings demonstrate selective patterns of atrophy across disease stages, with medial temporal lobe structures exhibiting early and pronounced volume loss relative to controls. These results support the hypothesis that Alzheimer’s disease progression is associated with non-uniform regional neurodegeneration and highlight the utility of large cohort datasets for identifying anatomically selective disease signatures.

articles.title: Questionable prospective effects between joint function and kinesiophobia: A comment on Wen at al. (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: In a recent study, Wen et al. concluded causal reciprocal effects between joint function and kinesiophobia based on findings from the cross-lagged panel model (CLPM). However, it is well known that effects in the CLPM may be spurious. Here, we reanalyzed data simulated to resemble the data used by Wen et al. We report discrepant positive, negative, and null effects of joint function on subsequent change in kinesiophobia, and vice versa, depending on the analyzed model. Meta-analytic aggregations of these divergent effects did not differ significantly from zero. Hence, we conclude that findings by Wen et al. may have been spurious and their conclusions premature. It is important to bear in mind that correlations, including cross-lagged effects, in observational (i.e., non-experimental) data do not prove causality in order not to overinterpret findings. We recommend researchers to fit, as we did here, alternative models to data and to base conclusions on a juxtaposition of findings.

articles.title: Leading Big Team Science as Early-Career Researchers: Challenges and Solutions
articles.authors: Rachael Miller; Megan Lambert; Drew Altschul; Vedrana Šlipogor; Julia Espinosa
articles.abstract: Big team science networks are large-scale, collaborative and global initiatives that pool intellectual and material resources to answer a common scientific question, while increasing transparency, inclusivity and replicability in research. In animal cognition and behaviour, these networks emerged in response to the replicability crisis, addressing limitations such as small sample sizes, taxonomic biases and restricted species and researcher representation. Examples of established animal-focussed networks include ManyPrimates, ManyBirds and ManyDogs, which collectively engage hundreds of collaborators across continents, countries, species and research sites. These initiatives have already produced empirical work, commentaries and reviews, demonstrating proof-of-concept and offering a model for comparative, cross-species science. While big team science offers opportunities for advancing scientific knowledge and promoting inclusive culture, leading such networks, particularly as early-career researchers, comes with challenges. We identify three key challenges shaping early-career researchers' leadership in big team science: 1. Power dynamics, “Reviewer 2” and challenging behaviour, 2. Communication, relationship-building and community trust in globally distributed teams, 3. Confidence, motivation and experience required to manage simultaneous roles as scientists, leaders and administrators. These issues can delay research progress, risk reputational harm and contribute to stress or burnout, particularly for early-career researchers navigating job precarity and career progression. We outline practical solutions, including transparent governance, clear authorship criteria, inclusive communication strategies, and the need for mentorship and early leadership training. We highlight the importance of securing funding to sustain long-term governance of big team science projects, and the vital role that senior researchers have in supporting, rather than overshadowing, early-career leaders. Ultimately, big team science complements traditional single-lab, PI-driven approaches, promoting inclusivity, diversity and collaboration over competition. Our collective experience suggests that big team science leadership, though it can be high-risk, is also among the most rewarding and transformative experiences of an academic career.

articles.title: Challenging Listening Conditions Promote Sequential Adaptations of Cognitive Control in an Auditory Flanker Task
articles.authors: Lauren Petley
articles.abstract: Flanker tasks are popular tools for studying executive function, notably conflict resolution – the suppression of an incorrect response in order to produce the correct one. Applying sequence analyses additionally reveals the time course of this cognitive control process. While such effects have been widely explored in visual tasks, very few studies have examined them in the auditory modality. Further, the influence of perceptually challenging conditions is unknown. The present report explores both of these effects using the data from a recent study that validated our auditory Directional Command Flanker Task. One hundred and forty-two healthy participants (aged 18 – 24) completed three different configurations of a masking version of the task, with a small (32.1 32 Hz), medium (93.1 Hz), or large (122.8 Hz) vocal separation. Another 62 healthy adults (aged 18 – 59) completed an abbreviated task with the largest vocal separation, as well as a visual flanker. The sequence effects observed in the abbreviated auditory task were identical to the visual task. However, all of these effects were nullified when target repetitions were omitted from the analysis. The masking version of the task revealed modulation by perceptual difficulty, wherein the least spectral separation invoked significant slowing after incongruent trials. Moderate vocal separation revealed an interaction between current and previous trial type. Eliminating target repetitions simplified these results such that only slowing following incongruent trials was observed, for one voice under the smallest separation, and one under the medium separation. Interestingly, these effects were only observed versus masking on the previous trial, suggesting adaptive changes in perception or selective attention. These results support a hybrid view of sequence effects that arise from both memory associations and changes in cognitive control.

articles.title: Investigating the Direct Effects of Selflessness and Self-centeredness on Relationship Quality: A Dyadic Study
articles.authors: Lucas David; Michaël Dambrun
articles.abstract: Health is increasingly conceptualized as a multidimensional construct encompassing both individual and interpersonal components, yet the interpersonal level remains underexplored. Drawing on the Self-centeredness/Selflessness Happiness Model (SSHM), this study examines how two modes of self-functioning (i.e. selflessness and self-centeredness) relate to relationship quality at both intra- and interpersonal levels. Across two dyadic studies (Study 1: N = 293 pairs; Study 2: N = 266 pairs), participants reported their levels of selflessness, self-centeredness, and multiple indicators of relationship quality (trust, satisfaction, commitment, and conflict). Actor-Partner Interdependence Models were used to test interpersonal effects. Results revealed consistent between-partner similarities across variables, suggesting affiliation processes or shared influences. Self-centeredness was reliably associated with lower relationship quality at both intra- and interpersonal levels, including lower partner-perceived trust in honesty across both studies. In contrast, selflessness showed limited intrapersonal associations but demonstrated positive interpersonal relations in Study 2, particularly with partner-perceived benevolence. Additional multigroup analyses indicated that these associations were largely invariant across relationship types, samples, and contact frequency. Overall, findings support the extension of SSHM to interpersonal domains and highlight distinct patterns of association between self-functioning and interpersonal health indicators.

articles.title: Dense repeated sampling reveals impaired hierarchical integration of metacognitive confidence that scales with depression
articles.authors: Paveen Phon-Amnuaisuk; Vanessa Teckentrup; Sol Fittipaldi; Robert J Crossen; Celine A Fox; Anna Hanlon; Sucharit Katyal; Stephen M Fleming; Claire Gillan
articles.abstract: Depression is consistently associated with metacognitive under-confidence in cross-sectional data. However, the temporal dynamics underlying under-confidence in depression remains unclear. We used ecological momentary assessments (EMA) to examine these dynamics and test candidate mechanisms underlying this relationship. Using a smartphone app, users completed twice-daily EMA assessments of depressive symptoms, and bi-daily assessments of a gamified metacognitive task indexing local (trial-wise) and global (block-wise) confidence. Multi-level vector autoregressive models across 162 citizen scientists (Mage=54.4 ± 11.3; 75% female) revealed higher overall between-person depression scores were associated with lower local and global confidence levels – an effect consistently observed at the individual symptom level. Within-person, depression scores significantly covaried with global, but not local, confidence, and cross-lagged analyses found no evidence for temporal precedence in either direction. Turning to mechanisms that might underlie under-confidence in depression, we found overall depression and symptoms to be associated with a diminished integration of local confidence (t-1) into future (t) global confidence (β=- 0.280, 90% CrI=[-0.465, -0.096], 𝐵𝐹=136). Moderated mediation analyses with depression at ±1 SD showed that this was mediated by a blunted autoregression of local-confidence (ΔIndex of Moderated Mediation (IMM)=-0.310, 90% CrI=[-0.533, -0.090], 𝐵𝐹=95). Computational modelling further revealed a more conservative confidence criterion in individuals with higher depression and its symptoms. Together, our results present a novel mechanism for persistent under-confidence in depression: individuals with higher trait depression require more evidence to report high local confidence, while having less stable local confidence fluctuations, resulting in weaker accumulation and integration into future global confidence.

articles.title: Recasting psychopathology mechanisms as traits
articles.authors: Whitney R. Ringwald
articles.abstract: Dimensional models of psychopathology describe the kinds of mental health problems people can have without explaining what maintains those problems. Explanation requires understanding mechanisms. Yet, this knowledge has been slow to accumulate due to lacking a framework to organize, measure, and compare mechanisms. I propose a path forward by recasting mechanisms as dimensional traits––individual differences in how people select, perceive, react to, and shape everyday situations.  A trait framework turns psychopathology mechanisms into constructs in their own right that can be taxonomized, measured, compared between people, and used to guide treatment decisions. I outline concrete steps to create a mechanism taxonomy, describe how to assess mechanisms with intensive longitudinal methods, then present current and future applications of this framework to advancing research and mental health treatment.

articles.title: Greenwashing Training Fosters a General Skepticism Toward Advertisements
articles.authors: Philipp von Sicherer; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: Training interventions have been proposed as a countermeasure against greenwashing. However, there is no clear consensus on their effectiveness. In an effort to contribute to a better understanding, we examined the impact of greenwashing training on consumers’ ability to discern greenwashing using an online survey experiment designed to resolve ecological and internal validity issues of past work (N = 518). Additionally, we extended the scope by exploring whether greenwashing training also affects healthwashing detection. Our study revealed that greenwashing training can make consumers more likely to perceive advertisements featuring deceptive environmental claims correctly as greenwashing but can also make them more likely to misperceive advertisements without such claims as greenwashing. Furthermore, it showed that although healthwashing is not the subject of greenwashing training, the triggered skepticism can transfer to healthwashing perceptions, too. Overall, these findings suggest that greenwashing training does not enhance greenwashing detection proficiency but rather promotes excessive suspicion.

articles.title: Smoking habits are associated with blunted cortisol response, but not with cortisol recovery in the Trier Social Stress Test-Online
articles.authors: Masaharu Ueno
articles.abstract:
Background: In Japan, alcohol, tobacco, coffee, and tea are categorized as “Shikohin” (i.e., non-nutritious items consumed for flavor). Shikohin provides a culturally grounded lens for examining health-related behaviors like smoking. Using it as a framework, I hypothesized that participants with high resilience and strong smoking habits would show blunted cortisol responses to the Trier Social Stress Test-Online (TSST-OL). Based on a previous study showing that individuals with high alcohol consumption and high resilience scores exhibited a specific blunted cortisol response, the purpose is to examine whether a similar relationship can be detected in smoking habits, which are also categorized as Shikohin.
Methods: Participants (N = 41) were classified into four groups based on the degree of smoking (≤ 14 or ≥ 15 cigarettes per day) and the median total score on resilience scales (low-resilience low-smoking, low-resilience high-smoking, high-resilience low-smoking, and high-resilience high-smoking). Participants completed the TSST-OL, measuring subjective stress and salivary cortisol, at eight time points. Exploratorily, a two-piece multilevel growth curve model with landmark registration (GCM-LR) examined the effects of smoking habits and resilience scores on cortisol concentrations. 
Results: Both the Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and the GCM-LR showed blunted cortisol responses and intact cortisol recovery in smokers. Differences in cortisol non-responder prevalence were not detected between the groups. All groups showed similarly elevated subjective stress responses. The likelihood ratio test in the GCM-LR model showed that the random effect of resilience scores was significant. 
Conclusions: The findings suggest that smoking habits are associated with a blunted cortisol response to psychosocial stressors, while not affecting cortisol recovery. It is possible that there are substantial individual differences in the effect of resilience scores on cortisol concentrations, which supports the multidimensional nature of resilience. Several patterns seemingly specific to smokers were observed: high baseline cortisol levels, blunted cortisol responses, and appropriate recognition of subjective stress. The relationship between smoking habits, cortisol, and resilience should be understood from a perspective that integrates cultural and social factors. Studying smokers may provide insights into the mechanistic distinctness of blunted cortisol response and resilience.

articles.title: White paper on the reliability of neurotechnologies in paediatric populations: a report from the Respect4Neurodevelopment UK network
articles.authors: Paola Pinti; Chiara Bulgarelli; Francesca Biondo; Matthew Bowdler; Sanjana Gandhi; Emil Ljungberg; Ciara Molloy; Ross E. Vanderwertr; Helene Vitali; Tracy Warbrick
articles.abstract: Neurotechnologies such as magnetic resonance imaging (electroencephalography (EEG), functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), and MRI/functional(f)MRI) have become popular tools for the study of typical and atypical brain development. However, while rapid developmental changes, age‑specific anatomy and physiology, and methodological constraints uniquely affect the stability of these imaging measures, there is no document or resource that synthesises these issues across modalities. This white paper from the Respect4Neurodevelopment UK Network explores how reliability is currently assessed in pediatric neuroimaging and identifies outstanding knowledge gaps. To this goal, we combined a community survey among neuroimaging users and developers with a systematic literature review. There was broad recognition that reliability is an essential factor for translating neurotechnologies into personalized or clinical applications (n = 39 respondents). However, from a total of 1245 screened papers across modalities, there were only 15 that explicitly assessed EEG reliability during development, 16 for MRI/fMRI and 2 for fNIRS. The assessment of reliability thus has yet to become routine practice in the developmental neuroimaging field, challenged by the lack of standardized procedures, competing academic pressures, and lack of time and funding. These limitations need to be addressed to ensure that measurements in younger populations are reproducible enough for robust inference, clinical application, intervention, and large scale cohort studies. Our work provides a roadmap for the methodological advances and standardization alongside coordinated community efforts and institutional support that are critical to advance the next generation of reliable developmental neurotechnologies.

articles.title: People Accurately Anticipate the Influence of Human and LLM Moral Advice
articles.authors: Neele Engelmann; Eva-Madeleine Schmidt; Ali Alhosseini; Ivar Rodríguez Hannikainen; Mengchen Dong
articles.abstract:
People are often skeptical of AI in the moral domain, yet AI-generated ethical advice can
influence moral judgments as much as human advice. We examined whether people
accurately anticipate this influence or specifically underestimate the impact of AI moral
advice. First, we developed realistic everyday moral scenarios and collected persuasive
advice from both incentivized human writers (N = 92) and GPT-4. AI-generated advice was
longer, more linguistically complex, more semantically uniform, more virtue-oriented, and
less extreme than human advice. Next, a new sample of participants (N = 390) either revised
initial moral judgments after receiving human or AI advice or indicated how much they
expected such advice to influence them. Contrary to the idea of AI-specific miscalibration,
participants were equally influenced by human and AI moral advice, expected similar
influence from both sources, and accurately anticipated the overall extent of that influence.
These findings suggest that abstract skepticism toward AI in moral contexts does not
translate into reduced influence or uniquely poor insight into that influence: people may be
influenceable, but they are not blindly swayed.

articles.title: Effects of Virtual Reality Learning Contexts on Foreign Language Words Production Behavioral and EEG Evidence
articles.authors: Cong Liu; Qiuxia Liu; Mengrui Zhu; Lu Jiao; Ruiming Wang
articles.abstract: While Virtual Reality (VR) has emerged as a promising approach to second language (L2) learning, its specific impact on L2 vocabulary production remains unclear. Combining VR technology with EEG, this study investigated the behavioral and neural effects of immersive VR contexts on L2 vocabulary production, compared to a traditional picture-word (PW) context. Behavioral results indicated that words learned in VR yielded higher accuracy during immediate testing, though this advantage dissipated in the delayed phase. ERP analyses revealed that VR-acquired words elicited smaller P200 and LPC amplitudes during the immediate test. Additionally, time-frequency analysis showed stronger μ-band suppression and reduced θ-band power under the VR condition. These findings indicate that VR contexts utilize sensorimotor simulation to enhance the embodied processing of linguistic input, facilitating subsequent vocabulary retrieval and production. This study provides strong electrophysiological and behavioral evidence for Embodied Cognition Theory.

articles.title: Prior Sensory Tuning Orients Error Processing During Sensorimotor Adaptation
articles.authors: Yi-Zhen Xu; Liang Lee; Shang Hua N. Lin; Yi-Cheng Lin; Ching-Po Lin; Li-Hung Chang
articles.abstract: Does heightened proprioceptive precision promote the more aggressive updating predicted by reliability weighting, or the more conservative updating predicted by source estimation? To address this question, we introduced an unperturbed tuning phase prior to prism adaptation, varying visual availability during reaching across three contexts: eyes closed (EC), eyes open with ambient vision masked (EO-), and eyes open with ambient vision (EO+). Proprioceptive uncertainty estimates revealed context-dependent tuning effects, with the strongest sharpening in EC, weaker and less consistent changes in EO-, and a trend toward increased uncertainty in EO+. To examine how these pre-tuned proprioceptive states shaped subsequent updating, we modeled prism adaptation behavior using a hierarchical Bayesian state-space framework. This analysis demonstrated that sharpened proprioception was primarily coupled with more conservative state updating, consistent with a source-estimation account. In addition, the model captured a complementary modulation of performance stability, where improved proprioceptive precision suppressed trial-to-trial fluctuations in movement. Both effects followed an EC > EO+ > EO- ordering, revealing a non-monotonic mapping between proprioceptive tuning magnitude and functional impact. This divergence suggests that the influence of proprioceptive sharpening depends not only on uncertainty reduction, but also on how visual availability organizes the sensory context during action. Collectively, these findings suggest that error interpretation is a state-dependent process shaped by prior sensory context, with sharpened proprioception biasing error evaluation toward more conservative updating.

articles.title: Neighborhood-level Factors Associated with Substance Use Among Hispanic Urban Youth
articles.authors: Igdalia Covarrubias Reyna; Paris B. Adkins-Jackson; Victoria Castillo; Kazi Priyanka Silmi; Nallely Segura; Aubrey Valenzuela; Precious Peters; Erika Pugh; Delfina Alvarez; Maribel Arias
articles.abstract: Multilevel factors across individual, family and neighborhood contexts influence youth substance use behavior. While previous studies produced mixed findings about how neighborhood factors affect substance use, few studies focused on the everyday experiences of Hispanic urban youth living in urban settings. This study examined how individual, family, and neighborhood experiences shaped the health, development, and substance use decisions among Hispanic urban youth. We conducted semi-structured interviews with Hispanic urban youth (ages 12-14) residing in urban neighborhoods across San Diego County. Guided by grounded theory and thematic analysis, with double coding, iterative discussions, and multi-phase processes, we identified emergent patterns, relationships, and themes related to neighborhood factors and conditions that shape youth’s decision-making processes and behavioral outcomes. We identified three multilevel contextual themes of influence as key determining factors for youths’ decisions to either “use” or “not use” substances: coping and knowledge occurring at the individual level, risks and supports at the family-level; and a lack of safe recreational spaces at the neighborhood level. Beyond the neighborhood, there are factors influencing substance use behavior among Hispanic urban youth in urban settings. Many of these neighborhood-level factors are tied to structural-level influences resulting from resource distribution and historical redlining.

articles.title: Inter-lexical inhibition is present early and has a unique developmental trajectory: evidence from a novel version of sub-phonemic mismatch
articles.authors: John Baltazar Muegge; Keith Baxelbaum; Abigail Fergus; Bob McMurray
articles.abstract: Lateral inhibition is a key property of spoken word recognition and has been implicated as an important factor in novel word learning and a locus of difference for some language disorders. Work on the development of lateral inhibition has produced mixed results, however, suggesting that it may not be fully operative in young children and has a protracted developmental trajectory. Here, we posit that these results have less to do with a lack of inhibition at these ages and is more a product of limitations of the common paradigm used to index inhibition, the sub-phonemic mismatch task. We introduce a variant of sub-phonemic mismatch which is able to detect inhibition at these early age groups. 236 children from grades 2-5 were given a multisyllabic word (battleship) whose initial portion could contain acoustic cues towards a nonword (bettle-ship) or another lexical item (bottle-ship). Eye-movements made across these conditions revealed that children have robust lateral inhibition even at young ages. Importantly, the strength of this inhibition is largely stable across ages. Further exploratory analyses suggest that development does occur, but in the timing or duration of inhibition instead of its magnitude. A follow-up experiment with adults replicates and clarifies this finding, suggesting a novel framework for understanding inhibition.

articles.title: Scalable, context-sensitive psychiatric assessment with large language models and brief diaries
articles.authors: Whitney R. Ringwald; Aman Taxali; Mike Angstadt; Colin Vize; Chandra Sripada; Aidan G.C. Wright
articles.abstract:
Background:  Accurate psychiatric assessment requires understanding a person’s unique experience within their psychosocial context.  Clinical interviews have been the gold standard for assessment as the only methods capable of this complex task, but they are time and resource intensive. Consequently, psychiatric assessment typically relies on patient report surveys that are decontextualized and narrow in scope.  This comprehensiveness-scalability tradeoff is a major bottleneck in studying and treating psychopathology. We propose using large language models (LLMs) to score psychopathology from brief personal narratives as a low-burden, context-sensitive solution.  

Methods: Participants (N=108) completed brief (~1 minute), freeform audio diaries daily for two weeks.  We used six LLMs to score wide-ranging psychopathology (Internalizing, Detachment, Disinhibition, Antagonism, and Anankastia) from the diary transcripts. Leveraging an array of self-report and clinical interview measures, we tested the convergent, discriminant, concurrent, and clinical validity of LLM ratings for both between-person differences and within-person fluctuations in psychopathology.

Results: Supporting convergent and discriminant validity, LLM ratings correlated most strongly with corresponding self-report domains at both the between (average convergent r = .42) and within-person (r = .28) levels.  LLM and self-report ratings had similar patterns of associations with external variables, except for Anankastia and Antagonism.  Further, every LLM-rated domain related to psychopathology ascertained by clinical interview.

Conclusions: Across multiple forms of validity, we showed that LLMs can assess most major forms of psychopathology from mere minutes of audio. These results support scoring open-ended narratives with LLMs as a scalable, portable method to translate idiographic diagnostic data into standardized psychiatric assessments.

articles.title: Are Positive Views of Authority a Privilege? Socioeconomic Status and Perceptions of Powerholders Across 80 Cultures
articles.authors: Arkadiusz Wasiel; Maciej Górski; Victoria Yeung; Ángel Sánchez Rodríguez; Mohsen Joshanloo; Eric Raymond Igou; Michael Harris Bond; Brian W. Haas; Heyla Sleim; Mladen Adamovic
articles.abstract: Public evaluation of those in power is fundamental to societal stability. However, how people judge powerholders depends on their positions within the socioeconomic hierarchy. Drawing on two large-scale cross-cultural studies encompassing 160 samples from 80 cultures, we examined whether socioeconomic status (SES) shapes how people appraise the motive of institutional powerholders. Though scepticism toward powerholders was widespread across the socioeconomic spectrum, higher-SES individuals consistently attributed lesser self-orientation and greater other-orientation to powerholders. In less corrupt societies, perceptions of powerholders were more favourable, and SES-based differences were more pronounced, indicating that the quality of national governance matters as well. In contrast, in highly corrupt national contexts, class-based distinctions largely disappeared. These findings suggest that viewing authorities as serving the common good may be both a privilege of one’s socioeconomic position and a privilege of living in a well-governed society. The results carry important implications for institutional legitimacy, social mobility, and the possible psychological foundations of populist movements.

articles.title: No Evidence of Experimenter Demand Effects in Three Online Psychology Experiments
articles.authors: Lucas Woodley; Xavier Roberts-Gaal; Rachel Calcott; Fiery Andrews Cushman
articles.abstract: Experimenter demand effects occur when participants alter their behavior to align with perceived study hypotheses, threatening internal validity. Concern about demand effects is pervasive in psychology. Experimenter demand may be especially acute in studies relying on experienced participants recruited online (e.g., via Prolific), who may readily guess hypotheses, or using common paradigms (e.g., vignette studies and interventions) where study goals are transparent. We conducted three preregistered experiments (N = 2,254) examining whether explicit demand cues influence online participants’ behavior across three paradigms commonly used in psychology: a dictator game, replicating prior work on demand effects (Experiment 1); a moral dilemma vignette (Experiment 2); and an intervention on group attitudes (Experiment 3). We randomly assigned participants on Prolific to receive information about the study’s hypothesis or to a no-information control. As expected, we find that receiving such information significantly shifts participants’ beliefs about the study’s hypothesis, creating an experimenter demand. Yet we find no evidence that learning any study’s hypothesis alters participants’ behavior, judgments, or attitudes, suggesting that demand effects may be elusive in online samples. These findings offer important insights for the design and interpretation of modern psychology experiments.

articles.title: Campus Greenspace Use and Student Well-Being: The Mediating Role of Perceived Stress and the Moderating Role of Nature Connectedness
articles.authors: Kritika Bakshi
articles.abstract:
The student population is witnessing a rise in psychological distress, although access to continuous and affordable mental health services is curtailed. The current study explored the degree to which the use of campus greenspace is associated with student well-being, considering perceived stress as a mediating variable and nature connectedness as a moderating variable. Based on Stress Recovery Theory (Ulrich, 1983) and the stress-buffering hypothesis (Cohen & Wills, 1985), this study promotes an ecological framework for mental health. A cross-sectional online survey was conducted among 106 University of Strathclyde students using validated tools: WEMWBS, PSS-10, CNS, and a composite measure of active greenspace use (NEACT).
SPSS version 29 and PROCESS version 5.0 were used to perform the analysis, with 5,000 bootstrap samples.

Greenspace use and the level of nature connectedness were signiVicantly correlated with lower stress and greater overall well-being. Mediation analysis revealed a partial indirect effect through stress, but the moderation analysis produced non-signiVicant Vindings. Threshold regression revealed no clear tipping point, but a positive dose– response pattern. Additional regression and visualisation supported the robustness of Vindings.


These results position greenspace as a non-pharmacological, scalable intervention with particular relevance for stressed university populations. Clinically, they support the integration of green social prescribing into student mental health services. From a policy standpoint, the Vindings highlight the need for biophilic design in campus settings and fair access to nature. Amidst the worldwide mental health epidemic among university
 
students, this study calls for the redesign of educational facilities, not just as places for learning, but as restorative settings that are attuned to human psychological needs.

articles.title: Fisher-Scale Variance Propagation for Artifact-Corrected Correlations
articles.authors: Piers Steel; Hadi Fariborzi
articles.abstract: Psychometric meta-analysis frequently corrects observed correlations for statistical artifacts such as unreliability and direct range restriction prior to synthesis. Prior work has already developed sampling-variance formulas for corrected correlations, most notably in the validity-generalization literature. However, those developments were framed primarily for corrected-correlation estimation rather than for Fisher-scale random-effects meta-regression. This note presents a Fisher-scale variance-propagation approach for artifact-corrected correlations. The method treats the corrected correlation as a deterministic function of the observed correlation, propagates sampling variance through that correction map using the delta method, and expresses the resulting study-level variance on the Fisher scale used for analysis. The estimator nests the standard Fisher variance when no correction is applied and can be used directly in conventional REML meta-regression. Preliminary simulations indicate that Fisher-scale variance propagation improves calibration of moderator inference relative to fixed-correlation approximations under heterogeneity and unequal study weights. This note is released to establish priority for this Fisher-scale reformulation prior to a fuller theoretical and empirical treatment.

articles.title: Neural Bases of Gesture and Spoken Language Processing in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder: An fNIRS Study
articles.authors: Bashar M. Farran; April Heien; Elizabeth Redcay; Stephen J. Sheinkopf; Laura M. Morett
articles.abstract: Children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) often show reduced comprehension of gestures during communication, suggesting atypical neural mechanisms underlying gesture–spoken language integration. The present study used functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) to investigate the neural bases of gesture and spoken language processing in children with ASD, with a focus on the inferior frontal gyrus (IFG) and posterior superior temporal sulcus (pSTS), key regions implicated in multimodal language integration. Twenty-four children aged 5–10 years (12 ASD, 12 typically developing [TD]) completed a gesture–spoken language categorization task comprising congruent gesture + spoken language, incongruent gesture + spoken language, gesture-only, and spoken language-only conditions. Neuroimaging results revealed robust condition-dependent modulation of oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO) responses in TD children, with a graded pattern reflecting increasing multimodal integration demands (incongruent > congruent > unimodal), formally confirmed by a significant linear trend contrast. Additionally, total hemoglobin (HbT) responses in TD children followed a similar pattern in the left IFG. In contrast, children with ASD exhibited globally attenuated task-evoked HbO responses and significantly reduced multimodal enhancement relative to TD peers, with a formal Group × Contrast interaction confirming that ASD children showed markedly less neural differentiation between multimodal and unimodal conditions. An initial channel-wise analysis suggested a numerically broader activation footprint in ASD under uncorrected thresholds, though this did not survive false discovery correction. Within the ASD group, reduced deoxygenated hemoglobin (HbR) responses during incongruent gesture–spoken language trials were associated with lower accuracy on social communication measures, reflecting reduced social communication performance.
