articles.abstract: In contemporary society, prediction models are extensively used to predict psychological outcomes, such as conduct problems, suicide, sexual reoffending, among others. Despite their widespread implementation, to the best of our knowledge, no studies have investigated model fairness from a causal perspective, elucidating discrimination pathways. The current case study aims to conduct a causal fairness analysis of a prediction model of conduct problems at age 14 years. Data were from the Longitudinal Study of Australian children, and the sample included adolescents (n=6,442) who participated in two follow-ups (ages 12 and 14). Conduct problems were measured with the Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire. The prediction model was developed with Minimax Convex Penalty logistic regression and discrimination was evaluated with the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) and the Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Fairness metrics such as demographic parity (DP) were used to detect potential violations. In the causal fairness analysis, the causal structure was discovered with the Direct Linear Non-Gaussian Acyclic Model algorithm, while fair adaptation with quantile preservation was used to generate fair data and train the fair prediction models. The findings indicate that, while the prediction model was discriminatory according to Indigeneity (DP=15%) and sex (DP=11%), a causally fair prediction model retained good discrimination (AUROC=0.74, AUPRC=0.43) while mitigating unfairness. In conclusion, this case study showed that prediction models can be discriminatory, reproducing structural inequalities captured in the data they were trained on, so careful fairness consideration and adaptation are required before societal implementation.

articles.title: A Community-led Intervention to Improve Well-Being among Latina Mothers and Caregivers
articles.authors: Monica Quezada Barrera; Igdalia Covarrubias Reyna; Maribel Arias; Delfina Alvarez; Kazi Priyanka Silmi; Erika Pugh; Aubrey Valenzuela; Precious Peters; Marybel R. Gonzalez
articles.abstract:
Latina mothers in the United States endure mental, physical, nutritional, and social disparities, patterned by systemic inequities. This study employed a community-led research design to promote well-being among Latina mothers residing in Southern California. Guided by grounded theory as methodology, meaning was constructed from the experiences of N = 20 participants
across two cohorts. Three salient themes were identified: (a) redefining health as holistic well- being, (b) reclaiming womanhood beyond motherhood, and (c) rooted in self, blooming for family. The findings indicated that community-led, culturally tailored programs meaningfully improve Latina mothers’ well-being and empower sustainable family health practices.

articles.title: To whom, why, and in what contexts adolescents share information about their substance use: A scoping review
articles.authors: Sophia Helena Blyth; Meghan Dontha; Tia C. Chitty; Herry Patel; Emily C. Kemp; William E. Pelham
articles.abstract:
Background: Obtaining an accurate understanding of adolescent substance use depends on adolescents’ willingness to voluntarily disclose substance use-related information. This scoping review synthesizes the literature on adolescents’ volitional sharing of information about substance use behaviors, attitudes, or experiences. 

Method: We followed revised scoping review guidelines to index articles in Scopus, PubMed, and PsycInfo from any time. We reviewed every peer-reviewed journal article we could find that assessed disclosure of substance use information in adolescents aged 12-17 years. Six clinical manuals for adolescent substance use treatments were also reviewed. 

Results: Systematic searches identified 17 studies that have addressed adolescent disclosure about substance use. However, only four focused directly on adolescent disclosure, as the remaining 13 studies touched on disclosure incidentally or tangentially. Only three of the 17 studies used a sample in which every participant was under 18 years old. All clinical manuals acknowledged the importance of adolescent disclosure in treatment, but few described typical disclosure patterns or provided guidance for obtaining disclosure. 

Conclusions: Basic information about disclosure, such as the percentage of times adolescents tell their parents or peers about their substance use, is unknown. We outline future directions for building a coherent evidence base on disclosure. A better understanding of adolescent disclosure of substance use information may enable parents, clinicians, other adults, and friends to better protect adolescents from early substance misuse and assist in reducing its short- and long-term consequences.

articles.title: Real-World Emotional Experience is Associated with Future Internalizing Symptoms
articles.authors: Paige Didier; Shannon Elizabeth Grogans; Jeffrey Harring; Kathryn A. DeYoung; Allegra S. Anderson; Samiha Islam; Jazmine Wedlock; Logan Craig; Christopher C Conway; Aidan G.C. Wright
articles.abstract: Depression, anxiety, and other internalizing disorders impose a staggering burden on public health. Alterations in emotional experience are central to theory, but prospective-longitudinal studies of real-world affect dynamics are scarce. Leveraging a hierarchical-dimensional approach, we examined associations between momentary emotional experience and broadband and narrow internalizing symptoms in a risk-enriched sample of 234 emerging adults followed for 2.5 years. Bayesian models demonstrated that low tonic levels of positive affect (PA) were uniquely associated with worsening Well-being symptoms, a core feature of depression, social anxiety, and trauma disorders. Baseline differences in tonic negative affect were associated with the severity of future anxious-arousal symptoms, but not with longitudinal change. Variation in reactive affect and exposure to everyday positive and negative events were unrelated to symptom trajectories. These observations highlight the centrality of tonic PA to the development of a key transdiagnostic symptom and set the stage for developing more effective intervention strategies.

articles.title: Young Children Engage in Choice-Relative Moral Reasoning
articles.authors: Talia Waltzer; Gail D. Heyman; Alan Leslie; Sydney Levine
articles.abstract: A central challenge in moral development is learning to evaluate actions not only by their outcomes, but also by the choices available to the person who acted. The present study investigates how this capacity develops in preschool-age children. Using a child-friendly version of the trolley problem, 3- to 5-year-olds (N = 124, 51% girls) judged a protagonist who moved a barrier to protect three children from having their cookies eaten by a mean squirrel. Children typically judged this action as morally good when no better alternative was available, even when it caused harm to another child. However, they judged the same action as morally bad when a non-harmful alternative was available but not chosen. Sensitivity to the availability of alternatives increased with age, indicating developmental change in how children integrate information about outcomes and choice constraints when forming moral judgments.

articles.title: The Safety Valve: A Mixture IRT Approach to Modeling Guessing Behavior
articles.authors: NA; Esther Ulitzsch; Lijin Zhang; Michael C. Frank; Benjamin Domingue
articles.abstract: Guessing behavior threatens the validity of psychological and educational measurement, yet standard corrections carry risks of their own. Ignoring guessing (via the Rasch model) can bias ability estimates, while modeling it (via the 3PL or 1PLg) risks instability when guessing is absent or misspecified. Contemporary alternatives (effort-moderated IRT, HYBRID models, hierarchical latent response models) require auxiliary timing or position metadata and can be complex to deploy. The two-class mixture IRT formulation we examine, in which one class follows a Rasch model and a second responds at chance, is a special case of formulations long available in the literature; our contribution is a quantitative case for treating it as a default. Using the InterModel Vigorish (IMV) across five simulation studies and four empirical assessments, we document an asymmetric pattern: when guessing is absent, the Mixture's cost is near zero (IMV ≈ 0.001); when present, it provides gains up to IMV ≈ 0.106. The 1PLg, by contrast, can produce losses an order of magnitude larger (IMV ≈ -0.116) when its guessing floor does not match the population. We derive a class separation condition that rules out two-alternative forced-choice formats. Item-specific guessing parameters provide negligible additional gains even when they generate the data.

articles.title: Artists' Drawing Strategies Serve to Overcome Visual Processing Limitations
articles.authors: Aaron Hertzmann; Judith Fan
articles.abstract: Most people learn to draw casually at an early age, but learning to create accurate observational drawings usually require extensive practice. Why is accurate drawing so hard? And what distinguishes people skilled at accurate drawing from those who are not?  Empirical research has primarily studied these questions for the task of copying a source picture. This article argues that the difficulty of copying tasks can be explained by limitations of visual memory and peripheral vision. In addition, variation in the control of eye movements could explain mixed findings regarding the relationship between perceptual skill and drawing skill, and regarding the role of prior knowledge in drawing. We explore the hypothesis that artists aiming to create accurate drawings from observation employ a broad suite of strategies for coordinating their eye movements with drawing actions, enabling them to transfer information from the source image to their drawing within the limitations of visual memory and peripheral vision. We advocate for further study of these strategies in naturalistic scenarios that extend beyond copying tasks.

articles.title: Emotional Synchrony in Family Networks is Linked to Adolescent Well-Being in Daily Life
articles.authors: Saida Heshmati; Jaymes Paolo Rombaoa; Courtney Allred; Alexander P. Christensen; Stacey N. Doan
articles.abstract: Family emotional processes play a central role in adolescent well-being, yet most research relies on dyadic or static approaches that do not capture the dynamic, system-level nature of family emotional life. In a sample of adolescents (ages 12–17), mothers, and fathers (N = 120; 2,365 assessments), constituting 40 family triads, we used ecological momentary assessment and a dynamic network approach, to estimate how emotions fluctuate over time and co-vary across family members within each system. Emotional synchrony—defined as the extent to which family members’ affective states change together—was modeled at both overall (between-family) and momentary (within-family) levels. Results indicated that families exhibited greater synchrony in positive affect than negative affect, alongside substantial heterogeneity in network structures. At the person level, greater positive affect synchrony was associated with higher adolescent well-being and lower distress, whereas negative affect synchrony showed inverse patterns. At the momentary level, increases in negative affect synchrony were associated with higher concurrent well-being and lower stress. These findings highlight emotional synchrony as a dynamic property of family systems and underscore the importance of modeling family processes across multiple timescales.

articles.title: Test Delay and Change Awareness Moderate Retroactive and Proactive Memory Effects
articles.authors: Samuel W. Cox; Colleen M. Kelley; James W Antony; Christopher N. Wahlheim
articles.abstract: Similar experiences can enhance or impair existing memories or newly encoded events. Such retroactive and proactive memory effects have been examined using A-B, A-D tasks in which pairs studied across two lists share cues but have changed responses. These tasks often show greater retroactive interference on immediate than delayed tests and greater proactive interference on delayed than immediate tests. Four experiments using A-B, A-D tasks characterized the role of change awareness in such effects. Retroactive interference from List 2 onto List 1 recall was observed on immediate but not delayed tests. When List 2 included measures of change awareness and List 1 recall, retroactive facilitation in List 1 recall was observed on a delayed test. Conversely, intrusions from List 1 onto List 2 recall, indicating proactive interference, were greater on delayed than immediate tests. Response interdependence on the final test showed that correct recall was associated with recollection of other list responses and that changes had occurred. These findings indicate that retrieval practice during List 2 both increased accessibility of earlier responses and promoted cross-episode integration. When the retrieved response was later the target, this accessibility produced retroactive facilitation; when it was the competitor, it increased proactive interference unless recollection of change preserved information about the responses’ temporal relationship. The theoretical implications are that temporal changes in retroactive and proactive interference depend not only on differences in trace accessibility across lists but also on whether study-phase retrieval promotes integrative encoding and whether change information remains accessible at test.

articles.title: Do Beliefs in a Negative (or Positive) Interdependence of Math and Verbal  Abilities Moderate Dimensional Comparison Effects? An Extended Replication Study
articles.authors: Lena Ott; Malte Jansen
articles.abstract: Dimensional comparison theory posits that students contrast their achievement across domains when forming academic self-concepts. A central assumption is that such contrasting processes are stronger when students believe that mathematical and verbal abilities are negatively interdependent (i.e., that one is either a "math person" or a "language person"). The present study (N = 2,323 German 10th-grade students) provides an extended replication of this moderation effect and introduces, for the first time, beliefs in a positive interdependence of abilities as a potential moderator. We first replicated the classic dimensional comparison pattern: Achievement positively predicted self-concept within domains (mathematics and German) and negatively predicted it across domains. Negative interdependence beliefs significantly strengthened these contrast effects. By contrast, positive interdependence beliefs did not attenuate cross-domain effects. These findings reinforce the role of negative interdependence beliefs as a central psychological mechanism underlying dimensional comparisons while raising questions about the conceptualization of positive interdependence beliefs.

articles.title: Emotional Synchrony in Family Networks is Linked to Adolescent Well-Being in Daily Life
articles.authors: Saida Heshmati; Jaymes Paolo Rombaoa; Courtney Allred; Alexander P. Christensen; Stacey N. Doan
articles.abstract: Family emotional processes play a central role in adolescent well-being, yet most research relies on dyadic or static approaches that do not capture the dynamic, system-level nature of family emotional life. In a sample of adolescents (ages 12–17), mothers, and fathers (N = 120; 2,365 assessments), constituting 40 family triads, we used ecological momentary assessment and a dynamic network approach, to estimate how emotions fluctuate over time and co-vary across family members within each system. Emotional synchrony—defined as the extent to which family members’ affective states change together—was modeled at both overall (between-family) and momentary (within-family) levels. Results indicated that families exhibited greater synchrony in positive affect than negative affect, alongside substantial heterogeneity in network structures. At the person level, greater positive affect synchrony was associated with higher adolescent well-being and lower distress, whereas negative affect synchrony showed inverse patterns. At the momentary level, increases in negative affect synchrony were associated with higher concurrent well-being and lower stress. These findings highlight emotional synchrony as a dynamic property of family systems and underscore the importance of modeling family processes across multiple timescales.

articles.title: Heartbeat Counting Task Performance in Alcohol Use Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Nicolás Guillermo González; Luisa Göde; Lucia Alba-Ferrara
articles.abstract: Interoception has been proposed as a key process in alcohol use disorder (AUD), and several studies using the Heartbeat Counting Task (HCT) have reported lower task performance in individuals with AUD than in healthy controls (HC). However, concerns about the construct validity of the HCT complicate the interpretation of these findings. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to quantify the magnitude and consistency of group differences in HCT performance between adults with AUD and HC, without assuming that the task provides a process-pure measure of cardiac interoceptive accuracy (IAcc). Eligible studies compared adults diagnosed with AUD with HC and reported sufficient HCT data to compute standardized mean differences. Searches were conducted in PubMed/MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Embase, and Web of Science, last searched on November 4, 2025. Six studies, including 314 individuals with AUD and 324 HC, were included. Study quality was indexed using an adapted Newcastle–Ottawa Scale. Standardized mean differences, expressed as Hedges’ g, were pooled using a random-effects model. Results indicated a moderate-to-large negative pooled effect (g = −0.79, 95% CI [−0.96, −0.63]), reflecting lower HCT performance in AUD. All studies showed negative effect sizes, although the small evidence base warrants caution. These findings identify lower HCT performance as a consistent task-level behavioral difference in AUD, rather than direct evidence of reduced cardiac IAcc, and underscore the need to determine whether this difference relates to clinically relevant features of the disorder. The review received no specific external funding and was preregistered in PROSPERO (ID = CRD420251173904).

articles.title: Understanding How Global Income Rank Comparisons Influence Charitable Giving
articles.authors: Harry D Coulson; Glen William Spiteri; Falk Lieder; Maximilian Maier
articles.abstract: People in high-income countries underestimate their affluence relative to the global population, potentially limiting their willingness to donate to charity. Across two Experiments, we investigate whether a rank-based nudge (RBN) informing US participants about their rank in the global income distribution (sourced from the World Inequality Database) increases donations to a charity helping people in less wealthy countries (Against Malaria Foundation). Experiment 1 (N = 1,217) found that the RBN significantly increased donations compared to the control condition. Moreover, participants in the RBN condition had more accurate assessments of the full global income distribution. An additional intervention, which elicited participants’ beliefs about how much people at different income ranks should donate to charity (injunctive distribution task, IDT) before receiving the RBN had no significant effect on donations. Experiment 2 (N = 1,998) corroborates the effects of the rank-based nudge found in Experiment 1 by showing that the effect replicates 1) when using non-PPP adjusted incomes, and 2) when including an additional income salience control condition, which asks participants for their income without providing a comparison to the global population. Experiment 2 also demonstrates the effectiveness of communicating relative income information across different communication formats, namely the median, the rank, and a combination of the two. These Experiments show that communicating ones’ position relative to the global income distribution is a useful method for increasing donations toward individuals who are less well off.

articles.title: Motivated Reading: Eye Movements Reveal Low-Level Avoidance of Attitude-Incongruent Information
articles.authors: Anat Lande-Brenner; Ruth Mayo; Yoni Pertzov lab; Noam Siegelman
articles.abstract: People tend to seek and engage with information that aligns with their pre-existing beliefs rather than challenges them, reinforcing ideological echo chambers and polarization. While prior research has focused on high-level choices (e.g., what people want to read and how they evaluate it), the present study asks whether motivated cognition manifests at the fundamental level of reading. Using eye-tracking, we monitored participants (N=76) - half vegetarian and half non-vegetarian - each reading short factual paragraphs about the positive and negative implications of vegetarianism, resulting in a total of 756 trials (i.e., reads of passages). Consistent with our hypothesis, there was a significant interaction between participants’ stance and the text’s argument on reading behavior: Readers skipped more words when texts opposed rather than aligned with their stance, indicating reduced cognitive engagement with attitude-incongruent content. This attitude-congruency effect emerged in both overall and first-run skipping measures. This research reveals that existing beliefs don’t just dictate what we read, they change how we read. By reducing engagement with incongruent content, this selective skimming acts as an underlying mechanism that creates a cognitive barrier, preventing new information from being fully processed. These findings suggest that motivated cognition operates already during early information intake, revealing a mechanism through which worldview-consistent biases take root.

articles.title: Mine or Yours? Electrophysiological Evidence of Interpersonal Variability in Conceptual Meaning
articles.authors: Ilenia Falcinelli; Maria Del Vecchio; Fernando Maggio; Laura Ferrari; Chiara Fini; Claudia Mazzuca; Doriana De Marco; Maddalena Fabbri Destro; Anna M. Borghi
articles.abstract:
People generally assume that others share their understanding of word meaning, despite substantial interpersonal variability in conceptual representations. The present EEG study investigates whether this is reflected in neural activity by examining the N400 component, classically associated with semantic integration processes. We also tested whether interpersonal variability differs across concept type by contrasting abstract concepts (e.g., philosophy), typically characterized by greater semantic variability, concrete concepts (e.g., candle), and ecological concepts (e.g., urbanization), recently proposed as having a hybrid nature between the two.
Nineteen Italian young adults first completed a free-listing task, generating semantic associations (targets) for abstract, concrete, and ecological prime words. In a subsequent EEG session, participants performed a feature verification task in which prime words were paired with either self- or other-generated semantically related targets. 
ERP responses elicited by the prime words were also analyzed to investigate the electrophysiological Concreteness Effect through the N400-like and N700 components.
Results revealed greater centro-parietal negativity for other- relative to self-generated targets in the N400 time window, suggesting increased semantic integration costs for externally generated meanings. This effect was not modulated by prime type. Prime analyses revealed a Concreteness Effect only in the N400-like window, with concrete and ecological concepts eliciting stronger and topographically distinct frontal negativities than abstract concepts, without differing from each other.
Overall, the findings suggest that the N400 component is sensitive not only to semantic incongruence but also to subjective differences in conceptual ownership, extending its role to interpersonal variability in meaning.

articles.title: Narrowing Boundaries: Mental Fatigue Reduces the Amount of Evidence that People Use when Making Decisions under Risk
articles.authors: Mara Bialas; Daniela Becker; Wolter Pieters; Eva janssen; David Vandemeulebroucke; Bregje Anne Marie Vosbeek; Erik Bijleveld
articles.abstract:
In workplaces, employees often have to choose between two options: following a time-consuming safety procedure, or taking a risky shortcut. Mental fatigue—common in modern, cognitively demanding jobs—may shape how people navigate these decisions. We examined this possibility in a within-subjects experiment (N = 43) in which participants repeatedly chose between a safe-but-slow option and a fast-but-risky option after a high-demand or low-demand fatigue-induction task. Overall, this fatigue manipulation did not strongly affect people’s tendency to take risky shortcuts. Yet, there were several indications that higher fatigue was associated with greater risk taking (i.e., experienced fatigue was associated with the tendency to take risk; risk-taking increased over time in the high-demand condition, but not in the low-demand condition). Using Drift Diffusion Modelling (DDM), we found that fatigue altered the decision-making process. Specifically, as predicted, in the high-demand condition, boundary separation decreased, indicating that participants took decisions based on less evidence. Together, these findings suggest that mental fatigue may not necessarily increase or decrease people’s overall tendency to choose risky shortcuts vs. safe options. However, fatigue may alter the decision-making process, such that decisions become less informed. These findings have potential implications for how to ensure safety in the context of mentally demanding jobs. 
Keywords: Decision-Making, Mental Fatigue, Risk Taking, Drift Diffusion Modelling

articles.title: The ARC-STR Model of Socially Transformative Resilience: A Qualitative Systematic Review and Meta-Synthesis Using Best Fit Framework Synthesis
articles.authors: Katherine S. Leventhal; Sumeet Jain; Fanie Collardeau; Claire Byrne; Vilas Sawrikar; Angus MacBeth
articles.abstract: One in seven adolescents worldwide experience a mental disorder. However, the converse is also important: in a world facing many crises, and even while navigating the changes of adolescence, six out of seven are not experiencing a mental disorder. This phenomenon has been called resilience. However, few conceptualisations of resilience have adequately accounted for the social forces that lead to marginalisation. This systematic review and meta-synthesis develops a transtheoretical model of Socially Transformative Resilience (STR). Best Fit Framework Synthesis was used, addressing the question: How do marginalised adolescents experience navigating challenges in a manner that builds both greater psychosocial wellbeing and increased psychological liberation? Fifteen databases were searched, with results situated based on the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme checklist. Studies were included if they focussed on marginalised adolescents, described first-person experiences of challenges and how these related to psychosocial wellbeing and psychological liberation, included analyses of primary qualitative data, were a published/peer-reviewed article, book/book chapter, PhD thesis, or unpublished report, and published on or before April 2, 2021 in English. Included studies examined lived experiences of 599 marginalised adolescents across 29 studies. The resulting ARC-STR Model, named for its upward arcing shape and its agentic components (awareness, reflection, choice), is one of the first to detail holistic mechanisms by which marginalised adolescents can be well while supporting changing unjust systems. This model describes how some adolescents combine these agentic components with self-in-world, wisdom, self-worth, humanist regard, and self-authorship, to build psychosocial wellbeing and psychological liberation. Focussing on these elements may help develop interventions, therapies, and supports to enable adolescents, regardless of their contexts, resources, or identities, to be well. Because this review focusses on marginalised adolescents and qualitative studies, future studies should examine how other age groups and those facing less marginalisation navigate similar concerns, including quantitatively. Registration: PROSPERO CRD42021231458.

articles.title: What are the Indecomposable Phenomenal Units of Qualia Structures?
articles.authors: Naotsugu Tsuchiya; Ryota Kanai
articles.abstract:
A central obstacle to a science of qualia is not only that qualia are subjective, but that we lack
a principled way to individuate them. Before asking which neural mechanisms generate qualia,
we must ask what counts as one quale rather than a compound of several experiential
elements. Here we propose that qualia, in the narrow sense, should be defined as
indecomposable units of phenomenal experience. This definition links qualia to perceptual
binding: some perceptual contents are automatically bound by genetically specified or
extensively learned neural circuits, whereas others require top-down attention to be transiently
composed. We argue that qualia, in the narrow sense, correspond to perceptual units that
remain structurally stable without top-down attention. Recent work on qualia structures
extends this proposal by showing that spatial attention can preserve or collapse the relational
structure among experiences. In particular, we discuss the results from a recent dual-task
experiment finding that withdrawing attention collapses qualia structures for geometric shapes
but not for faces. We will discuss how the concept of fundamental “unit” of experience may or
may not be compatible with the overall framework of the Qualia Structure paradigm.

articles.title: Co-produced research questions about dyslexia, dysgraphia, and dyscalculia: A collaboration between researchers and stakeholders facilitated by the UK Specific Learning Difficulties Network
articles.authors: Ailbhe McKinney; Alison Elizabeth Roulstone; Jo Van Herwegen; Michelle Luciano; Silvia Paracchini; Sinead Rhodes
articles.abstract: The Specific Learning Difficulties Network (SLDN) was established in 2022 to bring together UK researchers and stakeholders to foster an infrastructure and network for interdisciplinary collaboration and participatory research methods. The SLDN’s goal is to conduct research aimed at improving the quality of life of people with specific learning difficulties (SpLDs; dyslexia, dysgraphia and dyscalculia). This short report details a co-production research priority exercise where stakeholders (individuals with diagnosis, family connection, or community leaders) and researchers collaborated to co-produce research questions about SpLDs. These co-developed research questions can be used to guide future research of the SLDN or other researchers.

articles.title: Divergent Perceptions of Autistic Parents Among Healthcare, Social-care, and Judicial Professionals and Autistic Adults : A Participatory Study
articles.authors: Adeline Lacroix; William Vallet; Meng-Chuan Lai; Daniele Langloys; Magali Pignard; Jerome Brunelin; Marine Dubreucq; Caroline Demily
articles.abstract:
Background:
Autistic people can and do become parents, yet they often feel misunderstood. They fear being judged on their parenting abilities by professionals from healthcare, social-care, and even judicial systems, including the risk of unwarranted and disproportionate child protection investigations or interventions. Despite these concerns being frequently reported qualitatively, they have not been investigated with quantitative data.
Methods:
Using a participatory research framework, we co-constructed a clinical vignette and a set of questionnaire items in collaboration with autistic parents to ensure that the materials reflected their lived concerns and priorities. Professionals from healthcare, social, educational, judicial, and allied health fields as well as autistic adults were invited to complete the survey. We compared their perceptions of a fictional autistic mother, before and after diagnostic disclosure, as well as their views on professional competency regarding autism.
Results:
Although professionals do not hold overtly negative views, their perspectives diverge from those of autistic adults. Professionals perceived the fictional mother with high autistic traits more negatively than did autistic adults before knowing the diagnosis. In addition, judicial, medical, and paramedical professionals were less likely to identify autism than autistic adults. However, participants reported that diagnostic disclosure was somewhat reassuring and decreased their concerns about the mother’s psychological needs. Professionals rated their own competency in autism higher than autistic adults did when evaluating the professionals, although most professionals reported needing further training.
Conclusion:
These results provide support for autistic parents’ concerns about stigma. Divergent perspectives between professionals and autistic adults may be partly explained by a “double empathy problem,” where autistic and non-autistic individuals do not share the same reference frame for behavior. The findings underscore the importance of targeted training on autistic parenthood for all professionals, particularly those with minimal prior autism-specific training, to reduce bias and improve understanding.

articles.title: Experimental habit formation using smartphone applications: challenges in inducing devaluation with extended training
articles.authors: Maya Bar Or; Tom Schonberg
articles.abstract: Understanding habit formation in humans remains a fundamental challenge, partly due to the constraints of laboratory-based studies. This study investigated whether prolonged, real-world engagement with a smartphone-based task could induce habitual behavior. Across five pilot experiments (n=27-43; total n=177), participants were randomly assigned to either short (ST) or extended training (ET) condition using a gamified button-press task delivered via a custom web application on their smartphone. Habit formation was assessed through three complementary tests targeting hallmark features of habitual behavior: automaticity, inflexibility, and insensitivity to outcome-devaluation. While the ET group showed improved performance on automaticity measures, neither the outcome-devaluation nor the inflexibility tests yielded evidence of habit induction across experiments. Bayesian analysis supported the absence of robust group differences in insensitivity to outcome-devaluation. Our findings highlight the theoretical and methodological difficulties of studying habits in humans. More broadly, these findings invite a reconsideration of how outcome-devaluation procedures are used to assess habit formation. Although outcome devaluation remains a central and widely used paradigm, it may capture only part of the broader process through which stimulus-response learning develops. We synthesize these findings into concrete design recommendations for future smartphone-based habit research. All code and data are openly shared to promote replication and advancement in habit research.

articles.title: Agent and conversational context modulate perceived empathy in a Human-Voice agent interaction: An fMRI study
articles.authors: Hurshitha Vasudevan; Bhoomika Rastogi Kar
articles.abstract: We examined perceived empathy in a human-voice agent interaction and hypothesized that empathy shown by the voice-agent and human would be perceived by human participants and the situational context will affect the level of empathy perceived. Participants viewed eight videos while in the fMRI scanner across two contexts (plan-a-trip, visit-to-a-nutritionist) and four empathy conditions, and rated perceived empathy after each video on a 4-point Likert Scale. Results showed comparable perceived empathy from the voice-agent and human actor and greater level of empathy from the human actor while responding to human being empathetic. However, different neural regions supported empathy perceived from human (dorsomedial prefrontal) compared to the voice-agent (left superior temporal gyrus). The ‘trip’ context marked the equivalence in perceived empathy from human and voice-agent whereas higher empathy ratings from the human actor were observed across both contexts. BOLD activations were associated with mentalizing aspects of empathy for the trip context and affective empathy for the nutritionist context. Brain regions coding for cognitive-evaluative and affective empathy supported perceived empathy from the voice-agent whereas regions coding for affective empathy from the human actor in the nutritionist context. This study highlights the potential for a collaborative human-voice agent communication with implications for health-care.

articles.title: Intuition and Deliberation in Fluid Intelligence Tests: Two Possible Routes to Success
articles.authors: Laura Charbit; Esther Boissin; Wim De Neys
articles.abstract: Fluid intelligence, often assessed through abstract reasoning tasks, is typically assumed to rely on deliberative, effortful problem solving. However, recent findings within the dual-process framework of reasoning suggest an alternative possibility: individuals with higher intelligence may generate correct responses intuitively, without engaging in deliberation. To test this “smart intuitor” hypothesis, we conducted two studies using a two-response paradigm. Participants solved abstract reasoning matrices, first providing an initial response under time pressure and concurrent cognitive load to minimize deliberation, followed by a final response without such constraints. Results consistently showed that although correct final responses were primarily achieved through corrective deliberation, a substantial number of correct answers were already produced during the initial intuitive stage. Notably, over 20% of sound reasoners produced consistently correct intuitive responses and qualified as “smart intuitors.” These findings suggest that while deliberation remains the dominant route to correct abstract reasoning, it is not the only one. Intuition can also support successful performance on abstract reasoning tasks, at least for a subset of individuals. Our results call for a broader conception of fluid intelligence—one that recognizes the contributions of both reflective and intuitive processes.

articles.title: What Makes People Laugh and Cry? Ethnographic Evidence from 60 Societies
articles.authors: Roza Gizem Kamiloğlu; Floris Roelofsen; Disa Sauter
articles.abstract: Laughter and crying have deep evolutionary roots, and both share acoustic and functional features with homologous vocalisations in other mammals. Laughter and crying emerge early in development and recur across the lifespan, yet what elicits them across societies has not been investigated systematically. Whether their triggers vary with local ecology and culture is therefore an open empirical question. We address this using ethnographic records from 60 societies in the electronic Human Relations Area Files Probability Sample Files, examining cultural domains, specific elicitors, and immediate social settings. Laughter clustered in domains of social engagement and was elicited most often by humour and mockery. Crying clustered in domains of adversity and was brought on by grief, sadness, fear, pain, and hunger. The two behaviours nevertheless overlapped in three domains: early life and child-rearing, life histories and emotional life, and artistic and performative settings. The most frequent triggers were also the most cross-culturally consistent; rarer ones varied with local ecology and meaning.

articles.title: Period and Prejudice: Cultural Differences in Menstrual Health, Literacy and Stigma between South Asian and White British Women
articles.authors: Michèle Denise Birtel; Lucia Volpi; Aisha Mirza; Laura M König
articles.abstract:
Background: Attitudes towards menstruation are shaped by cultural and social norms and can result in misperceptions, impacting women’s health. Yet, research on cultural differences in menstrual experiences remains limited. 
Objectives: This study aimed to examine whether South Asian and White British women living in the UK differ in menstrual experiences, with a focus on menstrual health, menstrual literacy, stigma-related factors, and ethnic microaggressions in the healthcare context. 
Design: White British (n = 153) and South Asian (n = 153) menstruating women living in the UK participated in a preregistered cross-sectional online survey. 
Methods: Menstrual health was assessed via menstrual distress, symptom management, and hygiene behaviors. Menstrual stigma was measured through affective internalized stigma, internalized stigma of seeking help, secrecy, and avoidance behaviors. 
Results: In addition to differences in menstrual health (menstrual pain, symptom management, hygiene behaviors), South Asian women reported lower menstrual literacy than White British women. South Asians also reported greater secrecy as well as stronger avoidance behaviors related to discussing menstruation and engaging in daily activities while menstruating, but also more positive emotions towards menstruation. Additionally, they experienced more ethnic microaggressions in healthcare. Secrecy and microaggressions mediated the effect of ethnic group on self-stigma of seeking help. 
Conclusion: Our findings suggest cultural differences in menstrual health, literacy and stigma, emphasizing the need for inclusive health education and stigma-reduction interventions. Addressing menstrual literacy gaps and tackling stigma in family and healthcare settings may improve support for South Asian women in the UK.

articles.title: Remembering Affect Between Moments: Assessing Peak-End Effects In Continuous Affect Measures
articles.authors: Leonie Cloos; wolf vanpaemel; Eva Ceulemans; Peter Kuppens
articles.abstract:
In experience sampling studies, participants are intermittently measured with repeated
assessments, to track momentary affect changes. The data is collected in specific time
intervals, leaving information gaps between assessments. To capture affective changes
between assessments, we developed a continuous measure where participants
retrospectively draw their affective trajectories. This approach, while potentially more
complete, may be influenced by retrospective biases, such as the peak-end effect, where
intense or recent moments are remembered disproportionately. In an experience sampling
study 115 participants rated their momentary positive and negative affect six times daily,
drew affect changes between assessments, reported events, and completed one daily
high-intensity sampling session with up to six additional momentary ratings. We examined
the divergence of the retrospective continuous affect drawings from the momentary affect
and event ratings, respectively. Results showed that for positive affect, divergence between
drawings and momentary or event ratings decreased with higher intensity. For negative
affect, however, higher intensity increased the divergence, but this effect was moderated by
the recency of the momentary rating or event. Findings suggest that negative affect tends
to be reported differently in retrospective assessments compared to momentary evaluations
or event assessments. This divergence should be taken into account in future studies using
this method.

articles.title: Executive function scores are lower for 20-month-olds with an elevated likelihood of autism and/or ADHD
articles.authors: Ailbhe McKinney; Amanat Bansal; Maryna Nosyk; Devangi Maheshwari; Caitlin Croke; Naveena Cara Luthra; Francesca Anderle; Tony Charman; Gaia Scerif; Sinead Rhodes
articles.abstract:
Background: Executive function (EF) difficulties are a transdiagnostic feature of autism and ADHD, but the emergence of these difficulties is not well understood, nor are the influences of socio-economic circumstances and autistic traits. Prior research involving toddlers < 2 years is scarce and has yielded null findings, possibly due to measurement limitations. We address these challenges with a battery of EF measures combining parent report with performance measures. 

Methods: 110 20-month-olds at elevated likelihood of autism/ADHD due to a first-degree relative with autism/ADHD and/or showing elevated autistic traits (n = 62), or at low likelihood (n = 48), completed six EF performance tasks. Parent report of EF was collected with the Early Executive Functions Questionnaire (EEFQ). An EF composite score was derived from the intercorrelated EF measures. Emotion regulation was analysed separately. Autistic traits were assessed using the ADOS-2 and the Q-CHAT. A socio-economic circumstance (SEC) score was derived from parental education level, parental occupational status, neighbourhood deprivation, and family income. Preregistered hypotheses, specified separately in the preregistration, were tested in one regression model for parsimony. 

Results: 
Toddlers at elevated likelihood of autism/ADHD showed lower EF scores (likelihood β = −0.4, p = 0.04, R² = 0.04), but this difference was fully attenuated after accounting for autistic traits (ADOS-2 β = −0.46, p < 0.001; likelihood β = 0.05, p = 0 .79, R² = 0.21), with little additional contribution from SEC (β = 0.17, p = 0.10, R² = 0.23). Emotion regulation was also lower in the elevated-likelihood group (likelihood β = −0.69, p < 0 .001, R² = 0.12), was unrelated to autistic traits (p = 0.89), and was partly attenuated after accounting for SEC (SEC β = 0.35, p < 0.01; likelihood β = −0.51, p = 0.02, R² = 0.2). 

Conclusions: 
We have detected relative EF difficulties in toddlers with an elevated likelihood of autism/ADHD earlier than previously reported, likely due to our more comprehensive measurement approach. Our findings indicate that EF scores may be a useful transdiagnostic indicator of autism and/or ADHD as young as 20 months and highlight the potential value of early intervention.

articles.title: On the computational nature of emotions: insights from metareasoning and transformers
articles.authors: Jakub Onysk; Jiazhou Chen; Quentin JM Huys
articles.abstract: Emotions and affect remain enigmatic and unquestionably fundamental to our understanding of human health and illness. Affective states govern what we feel, think, say and do, but often seem to do so poorly. Why is this?  This paper examines the argument that temporally-consistent emotion-like heuristics or strategies might help the brain address the fundamental metareasoning challenge in time—what to think about and how much?  This view casts established signatures of emotions in cognitive psychology as shaping the brain's internal prioritization of computations in an approximately resource-rational manner. Language and narratives support this by contributing a temporal consistency that reflects a high-level conceptual heuristic selection.  Attention mechanisms and representations in transformer neural network models provide an intriguing implementation of this process and a potential avenue to study aspects of the metareasoning components of human emotions. This brings into focus a new computational view of what emotions are, why they exists and how they work, reconciling some of their objective and subjective characteristics. The paper closes with an outline of two applications, namely understanding self-report of mental-illness symptoms by quantifying the underlying affective states and understanding the neural representations of affective states.

articles.title: A Replication of the Impact of Digital Distractions and Semantic Associations on Short-Term Memory Recall
articles.authors: Benjamin Abalos Lorenzana; Sheynie N. Viernes; Erika Ruby T. Sor; Mariecris A. Cauan; Monalie I. Balani
articles.abstract: This study sought to replicate the methodological framework established by Kaleshevich (2022) to investigate how cognitive load from active distractions impacts short-term memory distortion. Grounded in load theory, the purpose of this replication was to examine the interactive effects of distraction type (literary reading versus social media usage) and semantic word association on the formation of false memories. A convenience sampling method was utilized to derive 16 second-year civil engineering students (N = 16), who were non-randomly assigned to four experimental conditions in a 2x2 between-subjects factorial design. Participants were exposed to a baseline list of neutral words, subjected to a five-minute distraction task using either an excerpt from The Great Gatsby or actively scrolling on Instagram, and subsequently presented with a secondary associated or non-associated word list. Descriptive data revealed that the social media paired with the associated word group produced the highest mean of false memory instances (M = 1.5). However, a one-way univariate Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) demonstrated that the differences between the group means were not statistically significant, F(3, 12) = 1.19, p = .355. Consequently, the study failed to statistically replicate the definitive findings of the original research. Both experiments yielded parallel descriptive patterns showing that social media exposure paired with associated word lists generated the greatest number of falsely remembered words, though this trend lacked statistical significance due to localized sample constraints.

articles.title: Workload as Coordinative Instability: A Dynamical Reframing for Human Factors
articles.authors: Carter Sale; Simon Hosking; Rachel W Kallen; Michael J Richardson
articles.abstract: Workload remains one of the central constructs in Human Factors, yet its dominant theoretical treatments continue to frame it as the occupation of limited internal resources. This paper argues that the main difficulty is not simply how workload is measured, but what kind of phenomenon it is. Resource-based models such as Multiple Resource Theory and Cognitive Load Theory have been productive, but they rely on indirect constructs, unstable category boundaries, and a processing architecture that becomes less convincing in dynamic, interactive task settings. As an alternative, this paper develops a coordination-stability account in which workload is defined as instability in the coupled agent--environment system: the erosion and reorganization of perception--action coordination under shifting task constraints. This framework is first developed conceptually through ecological psychology, coordination dynamics, and representative design. It is then evaluated against a resource-based account using findings from the Multi-Attribute Task Battery (MATB), where the two frameworks generate different expectations about performance, individual differences, behavioral dynamics, subsystem coupling, and workload measurement. The MATB results are more consistent with the coordination-stability view: performance changes are non-uniform and partly non-monotonic, workload signatures are stable within individuals but transfer poorly across them, behavioral dynamics reorganize under increasing demand, and movement-based measures outperform traditional task metrics in classifying workload. The paper concludes that reframing workload as coordinative instability resolves persistent anomalies in the literature, and provides a richer methodological and practical toolkit for Human Factors research, including representative task design, dynamical measurement, and interventions aimed at improving performance by supporting stable coordination.

articles.title: Increased reward sensitivity during motivational decision-making is associated with preserved perimenstrual mood
articles.authors: Annalise Whines; Sara Z. Mehrhof; Tory A. Eisenlohr-Moul; Camilla Nord
articles.abstract: The menstrual cycle involves hormonal fluctuations that can impact mental health. During the premenstrual phase, many people who menstruate experience the onset or exacerbation of low mood, anxiety, irritability, anhedonia, and somatic symptoms. We hypothesised that estradiol’s influence on motivational decision-making may underpin worsening mental health during the premenstrual phase. To test this, we tracked effort-based decision-making at two key points in the menstrual cycle using a well-validated physical effort-expenditure task and computational model. Two hundred naturally cycling participants from the general population completed the task and psychiatric measures in a counterbalanced order during their menstrual cycle’s periovulatory phase (marked by high estradiol and low progesterone) and perimenstrual phase (marked by low estradiol and low progesterone). As expected, mental health symptoms (including depression, anxiety, and anhedonia) worsened during the perimenstrual phase. Computational modelling revealed that the perimenstrual phase was characterised by increased reward sensitivity and decreased acceptance bias during effort-based decision-making. Importantly, those individuals who exhibited larger perimenstrual increases in reward sensitivity showed smaller increases in depression symptoms. These findings suggest that perimenstrual shifts in reward sensitivity in the general population represent a neurocognitive response to declining estradiol that may help buffer against low mood.

articles.title: A theory of memory for items and associations
articles.authors: Beige Jin; David Halpern; Jessica A. Wachter; Michael J. Kahana
articles.abstract: We present a retrieved-context theory of memory for items, associations, and their interaction (CMR-IA). Our theory assumes an evolving representation of temporal context that binds to items and associations, allowing the rememberer to make judgments based on the occurrence of a mnemonic target within a particular context. In addition to the assumptions inherited from prior retrieved-context theories, CMR-IA assumes a conjunctive (Gestalt) representation for paired associates, increased attention to rare items, and variable thresholds for recognition decisions. We apply CMR-IA to key findings concerning recognition of items and associations, including effects of recency, similarity, receiver-operating characteristic curves, word frequency, differential forgettings of items and associations, and contiguity effects for successive probes. We also apply CMR-IA to cued recall phenomena, including serial position effects, distribution of correct responses and errors, contiguity effects, associative symmetry, and similarity effects. Finally, we ask whether CMR-IA can account for the dependencies between successive tests of item and associative memory. We show that combining a Gestalt associative mechanism with retrieved-context theory provides a good account for many empirical phenomena concerning item and associative memory. The analysis of successive memory tests highlights the important role of output encoding in our model.

articles.title: How Interviewees Determine What Interviewers Want to Know
articles.authors: David A. Neequaye; Alexandra Lorson; Holly Kate Barnett
articles.abstract: We examine the mechanisms by which interviewees in investigative interviews mentally organize information when deciphering what an interviewer wants to know. The overarching idea is that such a process stems from the extent to which an interviewer’s question specifies an objective. Our initial test (i.e., Neequaye & Lorson, 2023) suggested two competing mechanisms: High-specificity questions lead interviewees to focus on particularly relevant details to the exclusion of other information, while low-specificity questions make interviewees focus on a broader range of information items (Mechanism-1)—versus—Interviewees generally assume that interviewers want to know all the information at their disposal, irrespective of question specificity (Mechanism-2). We conducted two conceptual replications to gain clarity (Replication 1, N = 318; Replication 2, N = 292). The results were similar across the board. The more specific the questions an interviewer posed, the more likely interviewees honed in on the details that should provide a pragmatic response to those questions. And interviewees’ disposition, whether cooperative, semi-cooperative, or resistant, had no effect on information-item designations. Contrary to our expectations, interviewees remained confident that they had identified what their interviewer wanted to know, irrespective of question-specificity.  This result held irrespective of whether the interviewer mixed high- and low-specificity questions (Replication 1) or consistently asked high- versus low-specificity questions (Replication 2). Thus, at this point in the research program, we lean more toward Mechanism-1.

articles.title: Advancing Dimensional Models of Psychopathology in Cancer: Insights from Applying the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP)
articles.authors: Darren Haywood; Jai Carmichael; Miriam K. Forbes; Roman Kotov; Robert Krueger; Aidan G.C. Wright; Susan Rossell; David Castle; Wendy Lam; Maryam Lustberg
articles.abstract:
Objective: Cancer survivors, defined as those living with or beyond a cancer diagnosis, experience more than double the prevalence of psychopathology when compared to the general population. Categorical diagnostic systems, such as the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), remain dominant in psycho-oncology despite concerns about reliability, validity, and clinical utility for this population. Dimensional frameworks, such as the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP), offer a more precise alternative; however, they have not yet been widely applied in cancer survivors. Accordingly, the objective of this research was to examine the applicability of HiTOP to cancer survivors.

Methods: Data from 1,389 participants in 28 countries (n=728 cancer survivors; n=661 community/psychiatric) were collected using the 405-item HiTOP-SR, alongside demographic, clinical, and cancer-specific measures. The HiTOP-SR normative sample (n=780) was also used. Analyses included parametric, non-parametric, and factor analytic approaches. 

Results: All HiTOP-SR scales demonstrated strong homogeneity and reliability in cancer survivors. Cancer survivors showed significant elevations across Internalising and Somatoform spectra, with current cancer associated with additional elevations in domains of Thought Disorder and components of Disinhibited and Antagonistic psychopathology. An 11-factor model was developed and supported for both cancer and community/psychiatric samples, though the magnitudes of the factor loadings sometimes varied between samples. External validity was strong with theoretically aligned associations.

Conclusion: The HiTOP-SR appears reliable within cancer survivors and provides utility in quantifying a broad array of psychopathology experienced. The results highlight the potential applicability and utility of HiTOP to improve cancer research and clinical practice in psycho-oncology.

articles.title: Identifying Interdependence Patterns in Triads Using the Triadic Actor-partner Interdependence Model
articles.authors: Shiyao Wang; Ginette Lafit; Evelien Schat; Eva Ceulemans
articles.abstract: Identifying and charting interdependence patterns at the system level is central to understanding the triadic interpersonal processes that take place in for instance mother-father-child triads across time. Possible patterns range from independence to triadic interdependence, over dyadic interdependence only. Although the recently proposed triadic actor-partner interdependence modeling framework (tAPIM) allows researchers to investigate how the different triadic partners impact one another, it does not directly provide inference regarding which interdependence pattern characterizes a specific triad. This study evaluates four strategies for identifying such patterns within the tAPIM framework: null hypothesis significance testing (NHST), equivalence testing (ET), and model selection using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) and the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). To systematically examine their performance, we conducted a simulation study assessing the Type I error rate, statistical power, and selection accuracy as a function of the number of measurement occasions per triad and effect magnitudes. Results indicate that NHST with multiple comparison correction and BIC outperform AIC and ET, and pinpoints conceptual problems regarding ET. We use daily diary data on emotional reactions of two triadic families to illustrate how to apply the strategies in practice.

articles.title: Gesture is Resistant to Symbolisation
articles.authors: Casey J Lister; Bradley Walker; Nicolas Fay
articles.abstract: Language is a defining feature of human cognition, yet its origin remains poorly understood.  Using an experimental-semiotic framework, we conducted two studies comparing the emergence of novel communication systems in the gesture and non-linguistic vocal modalities, while testing a dynamic model of language origin and evolution.  In Experiment 1, pairs of participants communicated recurring sets of words using either gestures or non-linguistic vocalisations.  Replicating prior research, communication success, behaviour alignment and sign motivation were substantially higher in the gesture modality, and success and alignment increased across repeated interactions in both modalities, supporting gesture-first theories of language origin.  Signs judged to be more motivated were associated with greater communication success, communication success predicted subsequent behaviour alignment, and behaviour alignment predicted later communication success, supporting the predicted interactions between cognitive alignment, behaviour alignment, and sign motivation.  Contrary to predictions, signs did not become less motivated (i.e., symbolise) over repeated interactions.  Instead, sign motivation increased across games.  Experiment 2 provided a behavioural test of sign symbolisation by examining whether naïve interpreters could identify the signs produced at different stages of interaction.  Consistent with Experiment 1, later signs were no less interpretable than earlier signs.  Together, the findings suggest that gesture supports the emergence of communication systems, but is resistant to symbolisation.

articles.title: From Traits to Moments: How Emotion Regulation Repertoires Shape Daily Affective Experiences
articles.authors: Joao F Guassi Moreira; Razia S. Sahi; Jennifer A Silvers
articles.abstract: Individuals possess stable repertoires of emotion regulation strategies, but little is known about how these trait-like patterns relate to momentary affective experiences in daily life. In this study, N=113 individuals completed a trait-level emotion regulation questionnaire at baseline and then participated in a 14-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) protocol capturing positive affect, negative affect, stress, and use of five emotion regulation strategies (reappraisal, suppression, distraction, selective attention, situation selection). Repertoires were operationalized in four ways: using three scalar indices that captured quantitative differences in questionnaire derived regulatory patterns and a fourth method relying on typological groupings that captured qualitative differences in repertoire construction. Hierarchical Bayesian modeling revealed broader, more extensive repertoire engagement was associated with lower negative affect and stress in daily life. All repertoire indices showed little conclusive evidence of moderating momentary regulation affect linkages. These findings suggest repertoire breadth confers modest affective benefits in daily life.

articles.title: Beyond Finsta: A mixed-methods study of behavioral dimensions of social media alternate account use
articles.authors: Xinyi Wang; Shuang Wang; Tao Xin
articles.abstract: Social media alternate account use is an increasingly common practice through which young adults manage audience boundaries and self-presentation on social media. Although growing research has examined the manifestations of alternate account use, existing findings remain scattered across platform and cultural contexts. Across two studies involving semi-structured interviews and survey data from 941 college students, Study 1 clarified the behavioral content and dimensional structure of social media alternate account use, and identified six prototypical behavioral dimensions, including emotional catharsis and venting, self-exploration and disclosure, daily recording, online relationship building, information sharing, and information acquisition. Based on these findings, Study 2 developed a 27-item scale. Exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses supported the six-factor structure, and additional analyses provided evidence for the scale’s reliability, validity, temporal stability, and measurement invariance. The findings provide a systematic framework for understanding social media alternate account use, which involves multiple self-focused and socially oriented practices. The measure offers a content-based and psychometrically reliable instrument for future quantitative research on this behavior.

articles.title: (Dis)Connected: Youth and Parental Expectations of Australia’s Social Media Ban
articles.authors: Sharon Horwood; Marina Torjinski; Shelley Hemmings; Hannah Jarman
articles.abstract: Background: Recent legislative changes in Australia meant that from December 2025, young people under the age of 16 were banned from accessing specific social media sites (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, X, Snapchat). Despite the considerable impact of this legislation, this is one of the first studies to explore of the perceptions and expectations of young people, and parents of young people, who are affected by the ban. Method: From June to August of 2025, 23 semi-structured online interviews of parents (n=11) and young people (n=12), including 8 dyads and 1 triad, aimed to capture the unique perspectives of the impending ban with respect to wellbeing, relationships, and the pragmatics of implementation. Themes and subthemes were derived via reflexive thematic analysis, and results were considered within the social change framework of Lewin’s Three-Step Change Management Model. Results: Participants reported a range of concerns, fears, doubts, hopes, and expectations with respect to the ban, most of which could be distilled into four main themes 1) There are holes in the bucket, 2) A necessary but challenging adjustment, 3) Balancing burden with support, and 4) Distancing of personal impact through social comparison. The themes and subthemes were able to be mapped reasonably well onto Step 1 and aspects of Step 2 of Lewin’s Change Management Model. Conclusions: Overall, the results demonstrate the complexity of effects that social media use can have on the lives of young people and within their family systems. A willingness to accept change as a means of longer-term betterment while still acknowledging the challenging nature of change indicated a maturity of viewpoint from young people, as well as empathy and understanding by parents. Learnings for implementation of similar legislation by governments are discussed.

articles.title: Curiosity in the Wild: Regularities in Naturalistic Information Seeking
articles.authors: Aditya Singh; Manuel D. S. Hopp; Alexandr Ten; Markus Spitzer; Aki Schumacher; Enkelejda Kasneci; Lisa Bardach
articles.abstract: Curiosity manifests in everyday information-seeking; however, most empirical research on curiosity relies on laboratory paradigms, limiting insight into how curiosity operates in naturalistic settings. Here, we study curiosity ‘in the wild’ using large-scale data from the online question-and-answer platform StackExchange (N = 60,154 questions). Drawing on curiosity theories, we identified theoretically motivated characteristics of questions, translated into behavioral measures, that were hypothesized to predict curiosity (conceptualized as the number of views a question received). For Science Fiction questions, question concreteness and familiarity contributed to higher curiosity. Semantic distance differentiated domains: less utilitarian topics (e.g., Science Fiction, history) favored semantic coherence in terms of proximity, whereas utilitarian topics (e.g., programming, web development) favored greater distance. Across topics, curiosity peaked at moderate uncertainty and increased with question importance (upvotes), suggesting domain-general patterns. Negative sentiment predicted higher curiosity only in non-utilitarian topics. Overall, naturalistic information seeking reveals organizing principles that extend existing curiosity theories.

articles.title: Social anhedonia predicts behavioral differences in young people with and without depression during social exchange
articles.authors: Brennan Delattre; Juliet Denise Griffin; Erdem Pulcu; Finley Watton; Amy Gillespie; Joshua Eusty Jonathan Buckman; Susannah Elinor Murphy; catherine harmer
articles.abstract:
The ability to form and sustain interpersonal relationships relies on the capacity to interpret social signals and update understanding of others’ behavior in dynamic contexts. Young adulthood is a critical period for refining these skills. Social learning and social reward responsivity have been implicated in depression, yet findings from social exchange studies remain mixed. Specifically, it remains unclear whether overall depressive symptom severity or specific symptoms such as anhedonia are most relevant to disrupted social decision-making.
In a single-session online study, we examined social learning and generosity sensitivity in a UK population sample of young adults (18–24 years; n=553). Participants completed the Social Learning–Generosity (SL-Gen) task, a multi-round trust game paradigm designed to probe feedback-driven updating and behavioral adaptation to structured generosity conditions, alongside questionnaire measures of depression (PHQ-9) and social anhedonia (ACIPS).
Depressive symptom severity was minimally associated with task performance; in contrast, greater social anhedonia and higher PHQ-9 anhedonia item scores were associated with differences in both evaluative updating and investment behavior. Specifically, individuals with greater social anhedonia showed lower initial investments, lower investments in higher generosity conditions, and smaller reductions in generosity ratings following unfair (loss-condition) interactions. Anhedonia was also associated with slightly flatter investment slopes in certain conditions.
These findings suggest that anhedonia, rather than global depressive symptom severity, is linked to reduced sensitivity to negative social feedback. Task-based social learning measures may therefore help identify mechanistic subgroups within depression characterized by blunted social reward processing which may have utility for pinpointing treatment targets.

articles.title: Self-compassion and mental wellbeing: a baseline cross-construct map from a multilingual Erasmus+ youth-sector sample in Greece and France
articles.authors: Anastasia Charitidou; Ioannis Valasakis
articles.abstract:
This is the companion paper to the design and feasibility evaluation of Compassion is Sexy (CIS), a 12–14 hour Erasmus+ KA210-YOU Small-scale Partnership compassion curriculum delivered across French-, Greek- and English-speaking workshop contexts during 2025–2026 (comPASSION is sexy, Project Code: 2024-3-FR02-KA210-YOU-000295806; no external research-ethics committee review; evaluation adheres to Erasmus+ quality standards for youth-sector practice). Where the intervention paper reports the dimensional pre–post pattern of change, the present analysis is restricted to the baseline phase and asks a single, prior question: how do the six factors of self-compassion relate to the three factors of mental wellbeing in this multilingual non-clinical adult sample?

The programme was delivered under the Erasmus+ KA210-YOU fewer-opportunities target framing; in 211 usable baseline participants (Greek site n = 105, including sizeable subgroups born outside Greece; French site n = 106, similarly multinational; roughly one third of respondents self-identified with at least one Erasmus+ fewer-opportunities or marginalised-status category), all six SCS-26 subscales were positively associated with the MHC-SF total (ρ = 0.18–0.34, all 95% CIs above zero except common humanity which crossed zero on the social cell). The strongest pooled pair was SCS total × MHC psychological (ρ = 0.42, CI [0.30, 0.52]). Across the 6×3 sub-by-sub matrix the pattern is selective rather than uniform: the self-relating SCS factors (self-kindness, self-judgmentR, isolationR, over-identificationR) load broadly across MHC dimensions, whereas the more cognitive-social SCS factors (common humanity, mindfulness) correlate reliably only with the psychological MHC dimension and show CIs that include zero for the emotional and social dimensions (Fig. 1, Table 1).

Baseline country contrasts indicate higher self-compassion in the Greek-site cohort (d = 0.45, CI [0.17, 0.74]) on SCS total and on four of six subscales, with MHC totals comparable; this baseline non-equivalence argues for a country covariate in any pooled mixed-effects reanalysis. The same architecture also held when the matrix was recomputed within fewer-opportunities (n = 69) and without fewer-opportunities status (n = 138) subgroups: no cell showed 95% CI separation, supporting the cross-construct map as a shared feature of the sample rather than an artefact of marginalised-status composition.

This is a baseline, observational, cross-sectional map; no causal or mechanistic claim is made. The architecture is compatible with a theoretical reading in which compassionate self-relating is more tightly coupled to eudaimonic (psychological) than to hedonic (emotional) wellbeing in this population, and provides effect-size anchors against which future multilingual deliveries can be planned.

Keywords: self-compassion subscales; MHC-SF; eudaimonic vs. hedonic wellbeing; cross-construct correlations; baseline; Greece; France

articles.title: Cross-Linguistic Factors in Early Numerical Development
articles.authors: Urvi Maheshwari; David Barner
articles.abstract: What role does language play in exact numerical reasoning? We argue that natural language represents number at multiple levels that vary cross-linguistically – in grammatical morpho-syntax, in number words, and in rule-governed counting routines. In this chapter, we first describe how cross-linguistic variability in grammatical structure impacts our representations of countable individuals and sets (Part I). Next, we review variability in how languages represent small numbers (Part II). And finally, we describe cross-linguistic variability in the syntactic structure of counting systems (Part III). We suggest that cross-linguistic research provides an important window into how humans come to reason about exact magnitudes, and conclude with a discussion of outstanding questions and avenues for future research.

articles.title: A short embodied compassion curriculum for the Erasmus+ youth sector: design, feasibility, and dimensional pre-post patterns of Compassion is Sexy in Greece and France.
articles.authors: Anastasia Charitidou; Ioannis Valasakis
articles.abstract:
Compassion is Sexy (CIS) is a 12–14 hour, four-session experiential curriculum integrating self-compassion practices, embodied and arts-based methods, and group dialogue for mixed adult audiences (youth workers, facilitators, NGO staff, young adults) in non-formal Erasmus+ settings. The programme was delivered across French-, Greek-, and English-speaking workshop contexts during 2025–2026 in Greece and France within the Erasmus+ KA210-YOU Small-scale Partnership project comPASSION is sexy (Project Code: 2024-3-FR02-KA210-YOU-000295806). The evaluation was conducted within a low-risk, practice-based youth-work framework incorporating informed consent, voluntary participation, anonymisation, and data-minimisation procedures. This is an uncontrolled observational feasibility evaluation; pre–post statistics reflect within-person associations rather than causal effects.

From 288 intake records across 23 site-level CSV exports, 223 unique participants were identified after email-based linkage, of whom 211 had usable baseline data (Greece n = 105; France n = 106) and 56 returned a usable post-form (Greece n = 50; France n = 6; overall completion 27%). Participants completed the SCS-26 and MHC-SF. Scoring used item means with reverse coding for negative SCS items and 50% prorating thresholds for MHC subscales. One French cohort of five participants recorded no psychometrics due to placeholder export headers, and three English-language forms omitted MHC item 7.

At the headline level, the programme was associated with a moderate positive shift in total self-compassion (Mpre = 2.98, Mpost = 3.31; Cohen’s dz = 0.54, 95% CI [0.30, 0.85]) and a smaller positive shift in total mental wellbeing (dz = 0.42, CI [0.17, 0.70]); the Greek-only subsample closely tracked the pooled estimates. Five of six SCS-26 subscales shifted in the direction of greater self-compassion (self-kindness dz = 0.52, common humanity 0.43, mindfulness 0.40, over-identificationR 0.44, self-judgmentR 0.38), whereas isolation showed no reliable change (dz = 0.12, 95% CI [−0.14, 0.39]). Within the MHC-SF, change concentrated in the social (dz = 0.45) and psychological (dz = 0.38) subscales, while the emotional subscale showed a smaller change with confidence intervals crossing zero (dz = 0.23, CI [−0.03, 0.49]). Attrition differed by age and number of comfortably spoken languages but was balanced on baseline self-compassion (d = 0.11, p = 0.46), suggesting that the observed SCS shift is unlikely to be fully explained by intake selection on the primary outcome.

These findings support the design and delivery feasibility of a short, embodied, multilingual compassion programme in real Erasmus+ youth-work settings and provide preliminary effect-size estimates for future controlled or waitlist-comparison studies.
Keywords: self-compassion · mental wellbeing · MHC-SF · Erasmus+ · youth work · non-formal education · experiential learning · feasibility study

articles.title: Reward Prediction Error Signaling as a Neurocomputational Correlate of Co-Occurring Anxiety and Depression in Youth
articles.authors: Marta Korom; Diana Burk; Tahjanee Givens; Daniel S. Pine; Melissa A. Brotman; Simone Haller; Bruno B. Averbeck; Ellen Leibenluft; Katharina Kircanski
articles.abstract: Co‑occurring depressive and anxiety symptoms (CADS) is a chronic and impairing condition that affects 1 in 6 youth. This underscores the need to identify neurocognitive mechanisms that characterize CADS. One candidate mechanism is altered reward prediction error (RPE), a reinforcement‑learning signal indexing the discrepancy between expected and received outcomes. Prior work has largely focused on RPE signaling in youth with anxiety or depression diagnoses, with limited attention to co‑occurring dimensional symptoms.
